That is what lets several of these chips share one set of bus wires: only the enabled chip drives the bus while the rest sit in Hi Z and stay out of the way.
Two enable pins turn the outputs on and off, and both are active LOW. "Active LOW" means the pin does its job when held LOW: pull an enable LOW and its outputs drive normally; let it float or go HIGH and those outputs switch to Hi Z.
  Yn = NOT(An) when the governing enable is LOW
  G=0, A=0 → Y=1 | G=0, A=1 → Y=0 | G=1, A=X → Y=Z (high impedance)

The 4 + 2 Split Enable
The two enables do not cover the same outputs. G1n (pin 1) controls the first four channels, Y1 through Y4. G2n (pin 15) controls the last two, Y5 and Y6. They act independently, so you can enable one group while the other stays in Hi Z.
This split is the whole reason the 74x368 exists as its own part. Its sibling the 74x366 is also a hex inverting buffer, but there both enables feed one gate and switch all six outputs together you get all six or none. The 368 instead gives you a 4 bit port and a 2 bit port in one package, each with its own enable.
The datasheet labels these pins to make the grouping obvious: 1G with inputs 1A1 to 1A4 for the group of four, and 2G with inputs 2A1 and 2A2 for the group of two. This guide uses G1n/G2n and A1 to A6 to match the simulator, where A1 to A4 belong to the G1n group and A5, A6 belong to the G2n group. The 74x367 is the same chip with non inverting outputs.

Common Uses
  - Driving a shared data or address bus: several chips wire their outputs together and the enables pick which one is allowed to talk.
  - Splitting one package across two buses a 4 bit group and a 2 bit group, each with its own enable.
  - Buffering and inverting memory-address or clock lines; TI built this family specifically as high-fan out bus and memory-address drivers.
  - Adding drive strength to a weak signal while also inverting it.

Gotchas
  - Hi Z is not LOW. A disabled output floats; it does not read as 0. If downstream logic needs a defined level while the buffer is off, add a pull up or pull down resistor.
  - The outputs invert. Y is NOT(A), not a copy. If you need a straight (non inverting) buffer with the same split enable, use the 74x367.
  - Enables are active LOW. Leave an enable HIGH (or floating) and its outputs stay switched off. Tie it LOW to turn the group on.
  - The split is 4 + 2, not 3 + 3. G1n covers Y1-Y4; G2n covers only Y5-Y6.
  - Enable just one driver per bus line at a time. Two enabled outputs fighting over the same wire (one HIGH, one LOW) is bus contention it wastes current and can damage the chips.
74x375:
The 74x375 contains four D-latches, each with complementary Q and Qn outputs. Latches 1 and 2 share clock C12; latches 3 and 4 share C34. When a C input is HIGH (transparent), the latch output follows D. When C goes LOW, the output is held.

Pins:
  1D: Data input for latch 1.
  C12: Clock for latches 1 and 2. HIGH = transparent.
  2D: Data input for latch 2.
  2Q: Output Q of latch 2.
  2Qn: Complementary output of latch 2.
  3Qn: Complementary output of latch 3.
  3Q: Output Q of latch 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  3D: Data input for latch 3.
  C34: Clock for latches 3 and 4. HIGH = transparent.
  4D: Data input for latch 4.
  4Q: Output Q of latch 4.
  4Qn: Complementary output of latch 4.
  1Qn: Complementary output of latch 1.
  1Q: Output Q of latch 1.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Paired Latch Clocking
The paired clock structure (C12, C34) allows two independent groups of two latches to be controlled separately. This is useful in nibble wide bus interfaces where two nibbles must be latched at different times.
74x376:
The 74x376 contains four J-NOT K flip flops sharing a common clock and common active LOW asynchronous clear (CLRn). J-NOT K means K is always the complement of J. When J=1 the flip flop is set; when J=0 it is reset on the rising clock edge. This simplifies single control set/reset applications.

Pins:
  1J: J input for flip flop 1. K is implicitly the complement of J.
  1Qn: Complementary output of flip flop 1.
  1Q: Output Q of flip flop 1.
  2J: J input for flip flop 2.
  2Qn: Complementary output of flip flop 2.
  2Q: Output Q of flip flop 2.
  CLK: Common rising edge clock for all four flip flops.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLRn: Asynchronous clear (active LOW). When LOW, all Q outputs go to 0 immediately.
  3Q: Output Q of flip flop 3.
  3Qn: Complementary output of flip flop 3.
  3J: J input for flip flop 3.
  4Q: Output Q of flip flop 4.
  4Qn: Complementary output of flip flop 4.
  4J: J input for flip flop 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

J-NOT K Operation
A standard JK flip flop toggles when J=K=1. The J-NOT K eliminates the toggle state by tying K to NOT J. The result is a D-like flip flop: J=1 sets Q=1 and J=0 clears Q=0 on the rising edge, without any hold previous state mode.
74x377:
The 74x377 is an 8 bit register: eight D type flip flops that share one clock and one clock enable. On each rising edge of CLK it captures all eight D inputs at once, but only when the enable (En, active LOW; the datasheet labels this pin G with an overbar) is LOW. With En HIGH the register ignores the clock and holds its value. Its outputs are always driven there are no tri state outputs like the 74x374 and there is no reset pin, so the enable is the only thing that decides whether a clock edge loads new data. Think of it as a 74x273 octal register with the asynchronous clear swapped for a clock enable, which is what you want in a register bank where each register loads only on its own write signal.

Pins:
  En: Clock enable, active LOW (labelled G with an overbar on the datasheet). LOW allows loading on the rising clock edge; HIGH freezes the register. It is sampled at the clock edge, so treat it like a data input, not a free running gate on the clock.
  Q1: Registered output for bit 1. Holds the last value loaded; always driven (not tri state).
  D1: Data input for bit 1. Loaded into Q1 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  D2: Data input for bit 2. Loaded into Q2 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  Q2: Registered output for bit 2. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  Q3: Registered output for bit 3. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  D3: Data input for bit 3. Loaded into Q3 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  D4: Data input for bit 4. Loaded into Q4 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  Q4: Registered output for bit 4. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock, shared by all eight flip flops (pin 11). Data is captured on the LOW to HIGH (rising) edge when En is LOW.
  Q5: Registered output for bit 5. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  D5: Data input for bit 5. Loaded into Q5 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  D6: Data input for bit 6. Loaded into Q6 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  Q6: Registered output for bit 6. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  Q7: Registered output for bit 7. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  D7: Data input for bit 7. Loaded into Q7 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  D8: Data input for bit 8. Loaded into Q8 on the rising clock edge when En is LOW.
  Q8: Registered output for bit 8. Holds the last value loaded; always driven.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

How the clock enable works
Each of the eight bits is an edge triggered D flip flop. On the LOW to HIGH transition of CLK, the value on that bit’s D input is copied to its Q output; between edges the output does not change. All eight flip flops share the same CLK pin, so the whole byte loads on one edge.
The enable decides whether an edge does anything. En is active LOW: when it is LOW the register is enabled and every bit loads on the next rising edge; when it is HIGH the edge is ignored and the outputs keep their old values. There is no separate reset, so holding is the register’s default behaviour whenever En is HIGH.
  En=1 (any CLK) → Q holds
  En=0, CLK rising, D=1 → Q=1
  En=0, CLK rising, D=0 → Q=0
  En=0, no rising edge → Q holds

The enable is synchronous, not a gated clock
A tempting way to build a load enable is to AND the clock with an enable signal. That works until a glitch on the enable makes a fake clock edge and loads garbage. The 74x377 avoids this: the enable is sampled at the clock edge, the same way the data is. TI states the part is designed to prevent false clocking caused by transitions on the enable input.
The practical result: treat En like another data input. It must be stable and correct before the rising edge, not just at the instant you want to load. On the LS part the enable needs roughly 25 ns of setup before the edge and about 5 ns of hold after it, and the clock runs to 30 MHz guaranteed. Those are the LS numbers and are simplified here; the exact values depend on the logic family (HC, HCT, F, and so on).

No reset, and how it compares to its neighbours
The 74x377 has no clear or reset pin. There is no single pin that forces the register to zero. To clear it, put zeros on the D inputs with En LOW and pulse the clock. If you need an asynchronous clear, use a different part.
  - 74x273 the same 8 bit register, but pin 1 is an asynchronous clear instead of a clock enable, and it loads on every clock edge.
  - 74x374 8 bit register with tri state outputs for driving a shared bus; it always loads on the clock edge (no enable).
  - 74x373 8 bit transparent latch: level sensitive rather than edge triggered, with tri state outputs.
  - 74x378 / 74x379 the same clock enabled register in narrower widths: 6 bit, and 4 bit with complementary outputs (each bit brings out both Q and its inverse).

Common uses
  - Parallel storage register: hold a byte and update it only on command by pulsing the clock with En LOW.
  - Register bank in a CPU or controller, where each register’s enable is its own write signal and the clock is shared.
  - Pipeline stage: capture a byte each clock, or freeze it by raising En, without adding gates to the clock line.
  - Buffer/storage registers and pattern generators (the applications TI lists on the datasheet).
74x378:
The 74x378 is a 6 bit version of the 74x377 clock enabled register, in a 16-pin package. En (active LOW) gates the clock to allow selective updating.

Pins:
  En: Clock Enable (active LOW). Pull LOW to allow clocking.
  Q1: Registered output 1.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  Q2: Registered output 2.
  Q3: Registered output 3.
  D3: Data input 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLK: Clock. Rising edge captures D1-D6 when En is LOW.
  Q4: Registered output 4.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  Q5: Registered output 5.
  Q6: Registered output 6.
  D6: Data input 6.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

6 bit Register
6 bit width is useful for 6 bit bus addresses, opcode registers, or any application requiring fewer than 8 bits. Same clock enable concept as 74x377.
74x379:
The 74x379 is a 4 bit clock enabled register with both Q and Qn outputs for each bit. This provides complementary signals useful in bus drivers and balanced transmission lines. En (active LOW) gates the clock.

Pins:
  En: Clock Enable (active LOW). Pull LOW to enable clocking.
  Q1: Output Q of bit 1.
  Q1n: Complementary output of bit 1.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  Q2: Output Q of bit 2.
  Q2n: Complementary output of bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLK: Clock. Rising edge captures D1-D4 when En is LOW.
  Q3n: Complementary output of bit 3.
  Q3: Output Q of bit 3.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  Q4n: Complementary output of bit 4.
  Q4: Output Q of bit 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Complementary Outputs
Having both Q and Qn available eliminates external inverters in differential drive or balanced push pull bus applications. The 74x379 also reduces component count when both true and inverted control signals are needed.
74x380:
The 74x380 is an 8 bit register with eight selectable operating modes (S0, S1, S2), tri state outputs (OEn), and a common clock. Modes include: hold, transparent latch, edge triggered register, shift right, shift left, parallel load, and clear. This single chip can replace several separate components in a data path.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, Q outputs are tri stated.
  Q1: Registered/latched output 1.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  Q2: Output 2.
  Q3: Output 3.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  Q4: Output 4.
  S0: Mode select bit 0.
  S1: Mode select bit 1.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  S2: Mode select bit 2.
  CLK: Clock. Clocked operations use rising edge.
  Q5: Output 5.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  Q6: Output 6.
  Q7: Output 7.
  D7: Data input 7.
  D8: Data input 8.
  Q8: Output 8.
  NC: Not connected.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Mode Selection
Three mode bits select among 8 functions: 000=hold, 001=transparent latch, 010=D flip flop, 011=D FF with OC, 100=shift right, 101=shift left, 110=parallel load, 111=synchronous clear. This flexibility makes the 74x380 suitable as a programmable data-path element.
74x381:
The 74x381 is a 4 bit arithmetic logic unit (ALU) that implements 8 arithmetic and logic functions selected by S0-S2. It also generates carry lookahead signals G (generate) and P (propagate) for use with a carry lookahead unit (74x182) to build fast ripple-free adders.

Pins:
  S1: Function select bit 1.
  S0: Function select bit 0.
  B0: B operand bit 0.
  A0: A operand bit 0.
  F0: Result bit 0.
  F1: Result bit 1.
  A1: A operand bit 1.
  B1: B operand bit 1.
  A2: A operand bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B2: B operand bit 2.
  F2: Result bit 2.
  F3: Result bit 3.
  A3: A operand bit 3.
  B3: B operand bit 3.
  Cn: Carry input.
  G: Generate output for carry lookahead.
  P: Propagate output for carry lookahead.
  S2: Function select bit 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

ALU Functions
S2:S1:S0 selects the function: 000=A minus B minus NOT(Cn), 001=A plus B plus Cn, 010=A XOR B plus Cn, 011=A XOR B, 100=A AND B, 101=A OR B, 110=A minus 1 plus Cn, 111=A plus A plus Cn.

Carry Lookahead (G/P)
G and P outputs enable a 74x182 carry lookahead unit to compute carries for all bits simultaneously instead of rippling through each stage. Connect G and P to the 74x182 to build a fast 16 bit or wider adder without carry propagation delay.
74x382:
The 74x382 is a 4 bit ALU identical in function to the 74x381 but replaces the G/P carry lookahead outputs with Cn+4 (carry out) and OVR (signed overflow detect). Use the 74x382 when you need a simple ripple carry chain and overflow detection rather than lookahead carry.

Pins:
  S1: Function select bit 1.
  S0: Function select bit 0.
  B0: B operand bit 0.
  A0: A operand bit 0.
  F0: Result bit 0.
  F1: Result bit 1.
  A1: A operand bit 1.
  B1: B operand bit 1.
  A2: A operand bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B2: B operand bit 2.
  F2: Result bit 2.
  F3: Result bit 3.
  A3: A operand bit 3.
  B3: B operand bit 3.
  Cn: Carry input.
  Cn4: Carry output (Cn+4). Connect to Cn of the next 74x382 slice for ripple carry.
  OVR: Overflow output. HIGH when a signed arithmetic operation overflows.
  S2: Function select bit 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Ripple Carry and Overflow
Connect Cn4 of the low order slice to Cn of the next slice for ripple carry addition. OVR detects 2s-complement signed overflow: if two positive numbers sum to a negative result (or vice versa), OVR goes HIGH. Cascade multiple 74x382s for wider arithmetic.
74x383:
The 74x383 is an 8 bit D type register with open collector outputs and an asynchronous clear (CLRn). It is functionally equivalent to the 74x273 but with OC outputs. Add external pull up resistors on all Q outputs.

Pins:
  CLRn: Asynchronous Clear (active LOW). Immediately clears all Q outputs to 0.
  Q8: Output 8 (open collector).
  D8: Data input 8.
  D7: Data input 7.
  Q7: Output 7.
  Q6: Output 6.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D5: Data input 5.
  Q5: Output 5.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock. Rising edge captures D1-D8.
  Q4: Output 4.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D3: Data input 3.
  Q3: Output 3.
  Q2: Output 2.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D1: Data input 1.
  Q1: Output 1.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

OC Register
Open collector outputs allow wired AND bus connections. Multiple 74x383s can share the same output bus line. Add a pull up resistor on each Q output line. The clear pin is asynchronous it operates independently of the clock.
74x384:
The 74x384 is an 8×1 bit two’s complement multiplier. It multiplies an 8 bit signed operand Y by a 1 bit multiplier X. When X=1 the 8 bit value passes through; when X=0 the result is 0. Because both operands are in two’s complement, negative Y values produce a correct negative result.

Pins:
  Y7: Operand bit 7 (MSB/sign bit).
  Y6: Operand bit 6.
  Y5: Operand bit 5.
  Y4: Operand bit 4.
  Y3: Operand bit 3.
  Y2: Operand bit 2.
  Y1: Operand bit 1.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Y0: Operand bit 0 (LSB).
  X: 1 bit multiplier input.
  P0: Product bit 0.
  P1: Product bit 1.
  P2: Product bit 2.
  P3: Product bit 3.
  P4: Product bit 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

8×1 Multiplier
Multiplying by a single bit simplifies to a conditional pass: if X=1 output P = Y; if X=0 output P = 0. This is the simplest building block of a shift and add serial multiplier. Cascade multiple 74x384s to build wider multipliers by summing shifted partial products.
74x385:
The 74x385 contains four independent serial full adder/subtractor units. Each unit accepts two 1 bit inputs (A, B), an add/subtract select (AS), and a clock. It produces a serial sum/difference output (S) with an internal carry flip flop chained to the next clock cycle. Useful for building bit serial arithmetic pipelines.

Pins:
  1A: Input A for unit 1.
  1B: Input B for unit 1.
  1AS: Add/Subtract select for unit 1. LOW = add, HIGH = subtract.
  1S: Sum/difference output for unit 1.
  2A: Input A for unit 2.
  2B: Input B for unit 2.
  2AS: Add/Subtract select for unit 2.
  2S: Sum/difference output for unit 2.
  CLK: Common clock for all four units.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  3S: Sum/difference output for unit 3.
  3AS: Add/Subtract select for unit 3.
  3B: Input B for unit 3.
  3A: Input A for unit 3.
  4S: Sum/difference output for unit 4.
  4AS: Add/Subtract select for unit 4.
  4B: Input B for unit 4.
  4A: Input A for unit 4.
  NC: Not connected.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Serial Arithmetic
Serial arithmetic processes one bit per clock cycle, LSB first. The carry or borrow is stored in an internal flip flop and used in the next cycle. Four independent units allow four separate serial arithmetic streams to run in parallel.
74x386:
The 74x386 contains four independent 2 input XOR (exclusive OR) gates. Output is HIGH when exactly one input is HIGH. It is functionally identical to the 74x86 and can be used interchangeably for parity generation, controlled inversion, and error detection.

Pins:
  1A: Input A of gate 1.
  1B: Input B of gate 1.
  1Y: XOR output of gate 1.
  2A: Input A of gate 2.
  2B: Input B of gate 2.
  2Y: XOR output of gate 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  3Y: XOR output of gate 3.
  3A: Input A of gate 3.
  3B: Input B of gate 3.
  4Y: XOR output of gate 4.
  4A: Input A of gate 4.
  4B: Input B of gate 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

XOR Gate Applications
XOR is true when inputs differ. Common uses: parity generation (chain multiple XOR gates to count odd/even 1s), controlled inversion (A XOR control output equals A when control=0, or NOT A when control=1), and comparators (XOR of two bits is 1 when they differ).
74x388:
The 74x388 is a 4 bit D type register with both standard and 3-state outputs. Q1-Q4 are always active; Q1n-Q4n are 3-state, enabled when OEn is LOW. This gives the chip flexibility to drive a bus via the 3-state outputs while also providing direct complementary signals.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable for 3-state outputs Q1-Q4 (active LOW). When HIGH, Q1-Q4 are tri stated. Qn outputs are unaffected.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  CLK: Clock. Captures D1-D4 on rising edge.
  Q1n: Complementary output 1 (standard, always driven).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Q4n: Complementary output 4.
  Q3n: Complementary output 3.
  Q2n: Complementary output 2.
  Q4: 3-state output 4.
  Q3: 3-state output 3.
  Q2: 3-state output 2.
  Q1: 3-state output 1.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Dual Output Architecture
The Qn outputs are always driven and provide the complement of Q. The Q outputs are 3-state for bus sharing. This allows the chip to simultaneously feed a bus (via Q) and a local logic block (via Qn) without external inverters.
74x390:
The 74x390 contains two independent 4 bit decade (BCD) counters with asynchronous clear. Each counter has two separate clock inputs (CLKA, CLKB) for the ÷2 and ÷5 stages. Connect CLKB to QA to form a full decade 0-9 counter. CLR (active HIGH) asynchronously resets to 0.

Pins:
  CLK1A: Clock A input for counter 1 (÷2 stage). Falls on negative edge.
  CLR1: Clear for counter 1 (active HIGH). Asynchronously sets QA1-QD1 to 0.
  QA1: Output A of counter 1 (bit 0, ÷2).
  QB1: Output B of counter 1.
  QC1: Output C of counter 1.
  QD1: Output D of counter 1 (MSB).
  CLK1B: Clock B input for counter 1 (÷5 stage). Connect to QA1 for BCD.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLK2B: Clock B for counter 2.
  QD2: Output D of counter 2.
  QC2: Output C of counter 2.
  QB2: Output B of counter 2.
  QA2: Output A of counter 2.
  CLR2: Clear for counter 2 (active HIGH).
  CLK2A: Clock A for counter 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

BCD Decade Counter
Connect CLK1B to QA1 to form a standard 0-9 BCD counter. The ÷2 stage (CLKA) counts 0-1 on QA; the ÷5 stage (CLKB) counts 0-4 on QB-QD. Together they wrap at 9.
74x393:
The 74x393 packs two separate 4 bit binary counters into one 14-pin chip. Each has its own clock and its own clear and shares nothing with the other, so you get two independent counters (or dividers) in a single package. Each one counts 0 to 15 on the falling edge of its clock and wraps back to 0. The clear is active HIGH and asynchronous: drive it HIGH and all four of that counter's outputs snap to 0 at once, without waiting for a clock edge. Inside it is a ripple counter each output stage toggles the next which keeps it small and cheap but means the outputs do not all switch at exactly the same instant. People reach for it as a frequency divider, a small event counter, or a step-sequence generator, and cascade QD into the next clock for longer counts. Its sibling the 74x390 is the decade (divide by 10) version.

Pins:
  CLK1: Clock for counter 1 (pin 1, labeled 1A on the datasheet). The count advances on each HIGH→LOW (falling) edge.
  CLR1: Clear for counter 1 (pin 2). Active HIGH and asynchronous: a HIGH forces QA1-QD1 to 0 immediately. Tie it LOW to let the counter run.
  QA1: Counter 1 output bit 0, the least significant bit (pin 3). Toggles on every clock edge (clock ÷2).
  QB1: Counter 1 output bit 1 (pin 4). Clock ÷4.
  QC1: Counter 1 output bit 2 (pin 5). Clock ÷8.
  QD1: Counter 1 output bit 3, the most significant bit (pin 6). Clock ÷16; cascade this into another counter's clock for more bits.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  QD2: Counter 2 output bit 3, the most significant bit (pin 8). Note counter 2's outputs run high to low down this side of the chip.
  QC2: Counter 2 output bit 2 (pin 9).
  QB2: Counter 2 output bit 1 (pin 10).
  QA2: Counter 2 output bit 0, the least significant bit (pin 11).
  CLR2: Clear for counter 2 (pin 12). Active HIGH and asynchronous, same as CLR1.
  CLK2: Clock for counter 2 (pin 13, labeled 2A on the datasheet). Counts on the falling edge.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

How It Counts
Each counter is four toggle flip flops in a chain a ripple counter. The clock feeds the first stage (QA); QA's output clocks the second stage (QB), QB clocks QC, and QC clocks QD. On every falling edge of the clock, QA flips. Each time a stage flips from HIGH to LOW it flips the next stage along.
The four outputs together read as a plain binary number, QA the least significant bit and QD the most significant. The count runs 0 (0000) up to 15 (1111), then rolls over to 0 and keeps going.
  Value = QD·8 + QC·4 + QB·2 + QA·1
  0:0000  1:0001  2:0010  3:0011  4:0100  5:0101  6:0110  7:0111
  8:1000  9:1001  10:1010  11:1011  12:1100  13:1101  14:1110  15:1111

Clearing and the Falling Edge Clock
Two details trip up beginners. First, the clock is falling edge: the count changes when the clock drops from HIGH to LOW, not when it rises. Second, the clear (CLR) is active HIGH and asynchronous "asynchronous" meaning it does not wait for a clock edge. The moment CLR goes HIGH, all four of that counter's outputs go to 0 and stay there until CLR returns LOW.
So to let a counter run freely, tie its CLR pin LOW (to ground). Pulse CLR HIGH whenever you want to reset the count to 0. Note this is the opposite of many chips, whose reset is active LOW watch the polarity when you wire it.

Cascading and Dividing Frequency
Each output halves the frequency of the stage before it. Feed a steady clock into the A input and QA is the clock ÷2, QB is ÷4, QC is ÷8, and QD is ÷16. That alone makes one counter a handy frequency divider.
To count past 15, chain the two counters: wire QD of counter 1 to the clock input of counter 2. QD ticks once every 16 input pulses, so counter 2 takes over each time counter 1 rolls over. The pair now acts as one 8 bit counter running 0 to 255 (a divide by 256). You can keep chaining across chips for as many bits as you need.

Ripple Counters Have a Catch
Because the stages clock each other in turn, the four output bits do not all change at the same instant on real hardware there is a tiny delay as the change ripples from QA up to QD. For a moment the outputs can show a wrong in-between number. Going from 7 (0111) to 8 (1000), for example, the bits flip one after another, so the outputs might briefly read 6, then 4, then 0, before settling on 8.
Usually this does not matter. But if you feed the outputs into a decoder or comparator that reacts quickly, those brief wrong values can produce short false pulses ("glitches"). When that matters, engineers use a synchronous counter (such as the 74x161 or 74x163) whose outputs all switch together.
Simplification: this simulator updates all four bits in a single step, so you will not see the ripple glitch here. It is a real-hardware timing effect the model leaves out.

Common Uses
  - Frequency divider or clock prescaler (÷2 up to ÷256 when cascaded).
  - Counting events or pulses in a simple project.
  - Generating an address or step sequence for scanning memory or driving a small state machine.
  - Fitting two dividers into one 14-pin package to save board space.
74x395:
The 74x395 is a 4 bit cascadable shift register with tri state outputs, a serial input (SER), parallel load inputs (A-D), clear (CLRn), and a load/shift mode select (SRn). When SRn=LOW the register shifts right; when HIGH it loads parallel data. OEn enables the tri state Q outputs.

Pins:
  SRn: Mode: LOW = shift right, HIGH = parallel load.
  SER: Serial input (enters at QA during shift).
  A: Parallel data input A.
  B: Parallel data input B.
  C: Parallel data input C.
  D: Parallel data input D.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, QA-QD are tri stated.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLK: Clock. Rising edge captures data.
  QD: Output D (tri state).
  QDn: Complementary output D (standard).
  QC: Output C.
  QB: Output B.
  QA: Output A.
  CLRn: Asynchronous Clear (active LOW). Immediately clears all outputs to 0.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Cascading Shift Registers
Connect QDn of one 74x395 to SER of the next for a longer serial shift path. The complementary output QDn is always active and used for cascading without needing the tri state QD pin.
74x396:
The 74x396 is an 8 bit storage register with open collector outputs. On the rising clock edge all eight D inputs are captured. Unlike the 74x374/373, there is no output enable outputs are always active (OC). Add pull up resistors on all Q outputs.

Pins:
  D1: Data input 1.
  Q1: Registered output 1 (open collector).
  D2: Data input 2.
  Q2: Output 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  Q3: Output 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  CLK: Clock. Captures D1-D7 on rising edge.
  Q5: Output 5.
  D5: Data input 5.
  Q6: Output 6.
  D6: Data input 6.
  Q7: Output 7.
  D7: Data input 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

OC Storage Register
Open collector outputs require external pull up resistors. This allows multiple devices to share the same output bus with wired AND logic.
74x398:
The 74x398 contains four 2-to-1 multiplexers, each with a clocked storage register and complementary (Q/Qn) outputs. A common SEL input selects between the A and B inputs for all four sections simultaneously. On the rising clock edge the selected input is latched.

Pins:
  SEL: Select. LOW = A inputs, HIGH = B inputs. Common to all four sections.
  A1: Input A of section 1.
  B1: Input B of section 1.
  Q1: Registered output of section 1.
  Q1n: Complementary registered output of section 1.
  A2: Input A of section 2.
  B2: Input B of section 2.
  Q2: Registered output of section 2.
  Q2n: Complementary registered output of section 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Q3n: Complementary registered output of section 3.
  Q3: Registered output of section 3.
  B3: Input B of section 3.
  A3: Input A of section 3.
  Q4n: Complementary registered output of section 4.
  Q4: Registered output of section 4.
  B4: Input B of section 4.
  A4: Input A of section 4.
  CLK: Clock. Captures selected input on rising edge.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

MUX with Storage and Complementary Outputs
The storage register prevents glitch propagation when SEL transitions. The complementary outputs eliminate external inverters when both true and inverted signals are needed.
74x399:
The 74x399 is the 16-pin version of the 74x398 quad 2-to-1 MUX with storage. It omits the complementary outputs but otherwise works identically: SEL selects A or B inputs for all four sections; rising CLK latches the selection.

Pins:
  SEL: Select. LOW = A inputs, HIGH = B inputs.
  A1: Input A of section 1.
  B1: Input B of section 1.
  Q1: Registered output 1.
  A2: Input A of section 2.
  B2: Input B of section 2.
  Q2: Registered output 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Q3: Registered output 3.
  B3: Input B of section 3.
  A3: Input A of section 3.
  Q4: Registered output 4.
  B4: Input B of section 4.
  A4: Input A of section 4.
  CLK: Clock. Captures selected input on rising edge.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Compact Quad MUX
Same function as 74x398 without complementary outputs. Use when only the true Q output is needed and package size is a priority.
74x401:
The 74x401 (9401 equivalent) is a CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) generator/checker for serial data. A 3 bit select code (S0 S2) chooses one of eight standard generator polynomials including CRC-16 and CRC-CCITT and their reverse (reciprocal) forms. Serial data is clocked in one bit at a time through D on the HIGH to LOW transition of CP; an internal 16 bit register accumulates the remainder. After the message, the ER output flags whether a transmission error was detected.

Pins:
  CP: Clock input. Data shifts through the polynomial logic on each HIGH to LOW (falling) transition.
  D: Serial data input.
  S0: Polynomial select bit 0.
  S1: Polynomial select bit 1.
  S2: Polynomial select bit 2.
  CWE: Check Word Enable. Held HIGH while data is entered; brought LOW to append (shift out) the accumulated check bits.
  P: Preset (active LOW). Asynchronously sets the register for the selected polynomial.
  MR: Master Reset (active HIGH). Asynchronously clears the register.
  Q: Serial data/check-bit output (most significant register bit).
  ER: Error output. Goes HIGH after the check frame if a CRC mismatch is detected.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

How CRC Works
CRC treats a serial data stream as a binary polynomial and divides it by a standard generator polynomial. The remainder (the CRC) is appended to the message at the transmitter. The receiver re-divides the data plus appended CRC by the same polynomial if the message is error free the remainder is zero and ER stays LOW.
The 74x401 selects among eight standard polynomials (CRC-16, CRC-CCITT, CRC-12, LRC-8 and their reverses) via S0 S2, giving good burst error detection for disk storage and data communication systems.
74x402:
The 74x402 is a programmable serial polynomial generator/checker. Unlike the 74x401 which uses only the CCITT-16 polynomial, the 74x402 accepts polynomial coefficients on P0-P3 and a GEN/CHECK mode select. It generates or verifies CRC for any 16 bit divisor polynomial of your choosing.

Pins:
  CLK: Serial clock. Rising edge shifts each data bit.
  DATA: Serial data input.
  GEN: Mode select. HIGH = generate CRC, LOW = check CRC.
  P0: Polynomial coefficient bit 0.
  P1: Polynomial coefficient bit 1.
  P2: Polynomial coefficient bit 2.
  P3: Polynomial coefficient bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  SYNn: Sync (active LOW). Start new frame.
  RESn: Reset (active LOW). Clears shift register.
  CEn: Chip Enable (active LOW).
  ERR: Error flag. Asserted when received CRC does not match.
  SO: Serial CRC output.
  STS: Status output.
  DR: Data ready output.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Programmable vs Fixed Polynomial
The 74x402 accepts custom polynomial coefficients on P0-P3 making it adaptable to CRC-CCITT, CRC-16, CRC-12, or application specific polynomials. The 74x401 is the fixed polynomial CCITT only variant.
74x403:
The 74x403 is a 64 bit (16 word × 4 bit) first in first out (FIFO) memory with tri state outputs, separate read and write clocks, and full/empty status flags. Data written at WR_CLK is independently read at RD_CLK, making it ideal for interfacing circuits running at different clock rates.

Pins:
  DIN0: Data input bit 0.
  DIN1: Data input bit 1.
  DIN2: Data input bit 2.
  DIN3: Data input bit 3.
  WR_CLK: Write clock. Rising edge writes DIN to FIFO if WR_EN is HIGH and FF is LOW.
  WR_EN: Write Enable. HIGH to write; data is lost if FF (full) is HIGH.
  RD_EN: Read Enable. HIGH to advance the read pointer on RD_CLK rising edge.
  RSTn: Reset (active LOW). Clears the FIFO to empty.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, DOUT0-DOUT3 are tri stated.
  MRSn: Master Reset (active LOW).
  EF: Empty Flag. HIGH when FIFO has no valid data to read.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  FF: Full Flag. HIGH when all 16 locations are occupied.
  RD_CLK: Read clock.
  DOUT3: Data output bit 3.
  DOUT2: Data output bit 2.
  DOUT1: Data output bit 1.
  DOUT0: Data output bit 0.
  IR: Input Ready. Indicates write side ready.
  OR: Output Ready. Indicates read side has valid data.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

What Is a FIFO
A FIFO (First In First Out) memory stores data in order and delivers it in the same order. Unlike RAM, there is no address you simply write to one end and read from the other. It is used as an elastic buffer between a fast producer and a slower consumer.
The EF (empty) and FF (full) flags prevent underrun and overrun. Always check EF before reading and FF before writing.
Note: Simulation note: the core FIFO is modelled write on rising WR_CLK (when WR_EN active and not full), read on rising RD_CLK (when RD_EN active and not empty), with working EF and FF flags and OEn tri-stating the data outputs. Not modelled: the RSTn/MRSn reset pins and the IR (input-ready) / OR (output-ready) handshake outputs, which are held LOW.
74x405:
The 74x405 is a 3 to 8 line decoder functionally equivalent to the Intel 8205. Binary address A0-A2 selects one of eight active LOW outputs Y0n-Y7n. Three enable inputs (E0n, E1n active LOW and E2 active HIGH) must all be asserted for any output to be driven LOW.

Pins:
  A0: Address bit 0 (LSB).
  A1: Address bit 1.
  A2: Address bit 2 (MSB).
  E0n: Enable 0 (active LOW). Must be LOW to enable outputs.
  E1n: Enable 1 (active LOW). Must be LOW to enable outputs.
  E2: Enable 2 (active HIGH). Must be HIGH to enable outputs.
  Y0n: Output 0 (active LOW). LOW when A=000 and all enables asserted.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Y1n: Output 1 (active LOW). LOW when A=001.
  Y2n: Output 2 (active LOW). LOW when A=010.
  Y3n: Output 3 (active LOW). LOW when A=011.
  Y4n: Output 4 (active LOW). LOW when A=100.
  Y5n: Output 5 (active LOW). LOW when A=101.
  Y6n: Output 6 (active LOW). LOW when A=110.
  Y7n: Output 7 (active LOW). LOW when A=111.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Intel 8205 Equivalent
The 8205 decoder was widely used in early 8080/8085 microprocessor systems to select one of eight memory or I/O chips by address range. The 74x405 is a direct TTL equivalent with identical logic.
Three enable inputs allow the chip to be enabled only when all conditions are met, enabling cascaded decoding without external glue logic.
74x407:
The 74x407 is an 8 bit data access register combining a D type register with a separate load strobe (LD) and an output enable (OEn). The LD signal controls when new data is captured from D0-D7; the CLK provides the clocking edge. CLRn asynchronously resets all outputs. Useful as a programmable I/O port.

Pins:
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  CLK: Clock. Captures data on rising edge when LD is asserted.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, Q0-Q7 are tri stated.
  LD: Load Enable. Must be HIGH during CLK rising edge to capture new data.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  CLRn: Clear (active LOW). Asynchronously resets Q0-Q7 to 0.
  Q7: Registered output 7.
  Q6: Registered output 6.
  Q5: Registered output 5.
  Q4: Registered output 4.
  Q3: Registered output 3.
  Q2: Registered output 2.
  Q1: Registered output 1.
  Q0: Registered output 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Load Enable Register
The separate LD input allows the register to hold its current value across many clock cycles, capturing new data only when LD is asserted. This is useful for bus width registers in microprocessor I/O ports where data should not change on every clock.
74x410:
The 74x410 is a 64 bit (16 word × 4 bit) static RAM with a registered (clocked) output stage and tri state outputs. The RAM array is written synchronously when WEn is LOW; the output register latches the read data on the CLK rising edge, holding it stable for downstream logic even as the address changes.

Pins:
  A0: Address bit 0.
  A1: Address bit 1.
  A2: Address bit 2.
  A3: Address bit 3 (MSB). Selects 1 of 16 words.
  DI0: Data input bit 0.
  DI1: Data input bit 1.
  DI2: Data input bit 2.
  DI3: Data input bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 9).
  WEn: Write Enable (active LOW). When LOW on rising CLK, DI is written to RAM.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, DO0-DO3 are tri stated.
  CLK: Clock. Rising edge latches RAM read data into the output register.
  DO3: Registered output bit 3.
  DO2: Registered output bit 2.
  DO1: Registered output bit 1.
  DO0: Registered output bit 0.
  CSn: Chip Select (active LOW). Deselect to disable all RAM operations.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 18).

Output Register Benefits
The output register isolates the data bus from address glitches. When an address changes, the RAM output may glitch briefly before settling, but the registered DO pins hold the previously valid data until the next clock edge.
74x412:
The 74x412 is an 8 bit multi mode latch functionally equivalent to the Intel 3212/8212 I/O port device. The MD (mode) input selects between output port mode (MD=0) and input port mode (MD=1). A strobe (STB) loads data and asserts an interrupt (INT) to notify a CPU that new data is available.

Pins:
  DS1: Device Select 1. One of two enables for the device.
  DI0: Data input bit 0.
  DI1: Data input bit 1.
  DI2: Data input bit 2.
  DI3: Data input bit 3.
  DI4: Data input bit 4.
  DI5: Data input bit 5.
  DI6: Data input bit 6.
  DI7: Data input bit 7.
  STB: Strobe. Rising edge latches DI0-DI7 and asserts INT.
  MD: Mode. LOW = output port, HIGH = input port.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). Tri states DO0-DO7 when HIGH.
  INT: Interrupt output. Asserted after STB, cleared when OEn is used.
  DO7: Latched output bit 7.
  DO6: Latched output bit 6.
  DO5: Latched output bit 5.
  DO4: Latched output bit 4.
  DO3: Latched output bit 3.
  DO2: Latched output bit 2.
  DO1: Latched output bit 1.
  DO0: Latched output bit 0.
  DS2n: Device Select 2 (active LOW). Must be LOW along with DS1 HIGH to select.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Intel 8212 Equivalent
The Intel 8212 was used extensively in 8080/8085 microprocessor systems as an I/O port and interrupt controller. In input mode the device presents data to the CPU; in output mode it holds CPU data for peripherals. The INT output drives the CPU INTR line.
74x413:
The 74x413 is a 256 bit (64 word × 4 bit) FIFO memory in a 16-pin package. Like the 74x403 (16×4 FIFO), it provides separate read and write clocks and empty/full status flags (EF, FF), but with four times the storage depth. No output enable outputs are always active.

Pins:
  DIN0: Data input bit 0.
  DIN1: Data input bit 1.
  DIN2: Data input bit 2.
  DIN3: Data input bit 3.
  WR_CLK: Write clock. Rising edge writes DIN when WR_EN is HIGH.
  WR_EN: Write Enable. Assert HIGH to enqueue data.
  RD_EN: Read Enable. Assert HIGH to dequeue data on RD_CLK.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RD_CLK: Read clock.
  DOUT3: Data output bit 3.
  DOUT2: Data output bit 2.
  DOUT1: Data output bit 1.
  DOUT0: Data output bit 0.
  EF: Empty Flag. HIGH when no data is available to read.
  FF: Full Flag. HIGH when all 64 locations are used.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

64-Deep FIFO Buffer
With 64 words of depth, the 74x413 can absorb bursts of data from a fast source before a slower consumer reads them. It is useful in serial communication buffering, printer spooling, and any producer consumer asynchronous interface.
Always check EF before reading and FF before writing to avoid data corruption.
74x414:
The 74x414 is an interrupt priority controller functionally equivalent to the Intel 8214. It accepts eight interrupt request inputs (R0-R7, where R7 is highest priority) and resolves which one should be serviced. It outputs a 3 bit encoded interrupt level (B0-B2), an INT strobe, and status outputs (OINT, PINT, INTB) compatible with the Intel 8080 CPU interrupt handshake.

Pins:
  EI: Enable Interrupt. When LOW, no interrupts are passed through.
  R0: Interrupt Request 0 (lowest priority).
  R1: Interrupt Request 1.
  R2: Interrupt Request 2.
  R3: Interrupt Request 3.
  R4: Interrupt Request 4.
  R5: Interrupt Request 5.
  R6: Interrupt Request 6.
  R7: Interrupt Request 7 (highest priority).
  CLK: Clock. Synchronizes interrupt sampling.
  SGS: Status Gate Strobe. Latches the priority status.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  INT: Interrupt output. Goes HIGH when a valid interrupt is pending.
  ENLG: Enable Latch Gate. Controls internal latch.
  B0: Encoded interrupt level bit 0.
  B1: Encoded interrupt level bit 1.
  B2: Encoded interrupt level bit 2 (MSB).
  OINT: Output interrupt signal.
  PINT: Priority interrupt output.
  INTB: Interrupt bus output.
  S0: Current serving level bit 0.
  S1: Current serving level bit 1.
  S2: Current serving level bit 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Intel 8214 Equivalent
The Intel 8214 was used in 8080 systems to manage up to eight interrupt sources. The highest asserted request wins and its 3 bit code is placed on B0-B2 for the CPU to read via INTA. S0-S2 communicate the currently servicing level to support priority masking.
74x416:
The 74x416 is a 4 bit bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs, equivalent to the Intel 8216. DIR controls the data direction: DIR=HIGH drives A→B, DIR=LOW drives B→A. OEn (active LOW) enables the outputs; when HIGH, all outputs are high impedance. Used to interface the Intel 8080 data bus.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, all outputs are tri stated.
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A data pin 1.
  A2: Side A data pin 2.
  A3: Side A data pin 3.
  A4: Side A data pin 4.
  NC: No connect.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  B4: Side B data pin 4.
  B3: Side B data pin 3.
  B2: Side B data pin 2.
  B1: Side B data pin 1.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Intel 8080 Data Bus Interface
The Intel 8216 (and its 74x416 equivalent) was the standard data bus driver for 8080 systems. The CPU data bus side connects to A1-A4 and the memory/peripheral side to B1-B4. The DBIN and WR signals from the 8080 drive DIR to control data flow direction.
74x417:
The 74x417 contains a modulo-2 (÷2) and a modulo-5 (÷5) counter stage driven by separate clocks (CLK2, CLK5) with shared parallel load (P0-P2, LD) and asynchronous clear (CLRn). Connecting CLK5 to the ÷2 output creates a ÷10 (decade) counter. The preload allows setting an arbitrary starting count.

Pins:
  CLK2: Clock for the ÷2 stage. Counts on falling edge.
  CLK5: Clock for the ÷5 stage.
  P0: Preload data bit 0.
  P1: Preload data bit 1.
  P2: Preload data bit 2.
  LD: Load. When HIGH, preloads P0-P2 into the counter on next clock.
  CLRn: Clear (active LOW). Asynchronously resets counter to 0.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Q0: Output bit 0.
  Q1: Output bit 1.
  Q2: Output bit 2.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Decade Counter Configuration
Connect Q0 (÷2 output) to CLK5 to get a BCD decade counter (0-9). The preload feature allows offset counting for example, start at 3 to count only 3-9 before rollover.
74x424:
The 74x424 is a two phase clock generator and driver equivalent to the Intel 8224. It takes a crystal oscillator (XTAL1/XTAL2) and generates the non overlapping two phase clocks (PHI1, PHI2) required by the Intel 8080 CPU. It also buffers the RESET and READY signals from the 8080 system.

Pins:
  XTAL1: Crystal input 1 (or external clock input).
  XTAL2: Crystal input 2.
  RESn: Reset input (active LOW). Drives the RESETn output after synchronization.
  RDYn: Ready input (active LOW). Drives RDYIN to the CPU.
  SYNCn: Sync from 8080 CPU (active LOW). Used for READY synchronization.
  TANK: Tank circuit input for crystal oscillator.
  OSCn: Oscillator output, inverted.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  PHI2: Phase-2 clock output to 8080.
  PHI1: Phase-1 clock output to 8080.
  RESETn: Synchronized reset output (active LOW) to 8080.
  RDYIN: Ready output to 8080 CPU READY pin.
  OSC: Oscillator output (non inverted).
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Two Phase 8080 Clock
The Intel 8080 requires two non overlapping clocks (PHI1 and PHI2) to sequence its internal pipeline. The 8224 (74x424) generates these from a crystal and ensures they never overlap if both were HIGH simultaneously, bus contention would destroy data.
74x425:
The 74x425 contains four non inverting tri state buffers. Each buffer has its own independent active LOW enable (E1n-E4n). When an enable is HIGH, that output is high impedance. Commonly used to selectively drive shared bus lines.

Pins:
  E1n: Enable for buffer 1 (active LOW). When HIGH, Y1 is tri stated.
  A1: Input for buffer 1.
  Y1: Tri state output 1.
  E2n: Enable for buffer 2 (active LOW).
  A2: Input for buffer 2.
  Y2: Tri state output 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  Y3: Tri state output 3.
  A3: Input for buffer 3.
  E3n: Enable for buffer 3 (active LOW).
  Y4: Tri state output 4.
  A4: Input for buffer 4.
  E4n: Enable for buffer 4 (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

Independent Enable Buffers
Each buffer can be independently enabled or tri stated. This is useful when four different bus lines need gating control from four separate signals rather than sharing a single OEn.
74x426:
The 74x426 is the active HIGH enable counterpart of the 74x425. Each of the four tri state non inverting buffers is independently enabled by an active HIGH enable input (E1-E4). When an enable is LOW, that output is high impedance.

Pins:
  E1: Enable for buffer 1 (active HIGH). When LOW, Y1 is tri stated.
  A1: Input for buffer 1.
  Y1: Tri state output 1.
  E2: Enable for buffer 2 (active HIGH).
  A2: Input for buffer 2.
  Y2: Tri state output 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  Y3: Tri state output 3.
  A3: Input for buffer 3.
  E3: Enable for buffer 3 (active HIGH).
  Y4: Tri state output 4.
  A4: Input for buffer 4.
  E4: Enable for buffer 4 (active HIGH).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

74x425 vs 74x426
The 74x425 and 74x426 are functionally identical except for enable polarity. Use 74x426 when the control signals are active HIGH (e.g., from TTL outputs that are already at logic-1 to enable).
74x432:
The 74x432 is the tri state output version of the 74x412 multi mode 8 bit latch. Like the 74x412 it replicates Intel 8212 behavior (strobe loaded, mode selectable, interrupt generating), but instead of open collector outputs it provides full totem pole tri state outputs controlled by OEn.

Pins:
  DS1: Device Select 1 (active HIGH). Assert to enable the device.
  DI0: Data input bit 0.
  DI1: Data input bit 1.
  DI2: Data input bit 2.
  DI3: Data input bit 3.
  DI4: Data input bit 4.
  DI5: Data input bit 5.
  DI6: Data input bit 6.
  DI7: Data input bit 7.
  STB: Strobe. Rising edge captures DI0-DI7 and asserts INT.
  MD: Mode. LOW = output port mode, HIGH = input port mode.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW tri state). When HIGH, DO outputs are high impedance.
  INT: Interrupt. Pulses HIGH after STB to notify CPU of new data.
  DO7: Latched output bit 7.
  DO6: Latched output bit 6.
  DO5: Latched output bit 5.
  DO4: Latched output bit 4.
  DO3: Latched output bit 3.
  DO2: Latched output bit 2.
  DO1: Latched output bit 1.
  DO0: Latched output bit 0.
  DS2n: Device Select 2 (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x412 vs 74x432
Both chips implement the Intel 8212 function. The 74x432 uses tri state (totem pole) outputs instead of open collector, making it faster and not requiring external pull up resistors.
74x433:
The 74x433 is the tri state output version of the 74x413 (64 word × 4 bit FIFO). It adds an output enable pin (OEn) that tri states the data outputs, allowing multiple FIFOs to share a common output bus. Otherwise operation is identical: independent read and write clocks, EF and FF status flags.

Pins:
  DIN0: Data input bit 0.
  DIN1: Data input bit 1.
  DIN2: Data input bit 2.
  DIN3: Data input bit 3.
  WR_CLK: Write clock. Rising edge enqueues DIN when WR_EN is HIGH.
  WR_EN: Write Enable (active HIGH).
  RD_EN: Read Enable (active HIGH). Advances read pointer on RD_CLK.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, DOUT outputs are tri stated.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  EF: Empty Flag. HIGH when FIFO is empty.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  FF: Full Flag. HIGH when all 64 locations are used.
  RD_CLK: Read clock.
  DOUT3: Data output bit 3.
  DOUT2: Data output bit 2.
  DOUT1: Data output bit 1.
  DOUT0: Data output bit 0.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Tri State vs Always Active FIFO
Use the 74x433 when multiple FIFOs share a common read bus. Only one device asserts OEn LOW at a time. The 74x413 (no OEn) is simpler when the FIFO is the only driver on the output lines.
74x436:
The 74x436 is a hex non inverting line driver designed to interface TTL logic with MOS memory buses. It features internal damping resistors on the outputs to suppress transmission line reflections on long PCB traces, reducing ringing that could cause false triggering of MOS inputs.

Pins:
  A1: Input 1.
  Y1: Buffered output 1 (damped).
  A2: Input 2.
  Y2: Buffered output 2 (damped).
  A3: Input 3.
  Y3: Buffered output 3 (damped).
  A4: Input 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Y4: Buffered output 4 (damped).
  A5: Input 5.
  Y5: Buffered output 5 (damped).
  A6: Input 6.
  Y6: Buffered output 6 (damped).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, all outputs are tri stated.
  EN: Additional enable input.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Damped Line Driver
The on chip damping resistors series terminate the output, absorbing signal reflections from capacitive or mismatched loads. This was important when driving long traces to DRAM chips in late-1970s minicomputer memory boards.
74x437:
The 74x437 is a hex non inverting line driver for MOS memory interfaces, identical to the 74x436 but without the internal damping resistors. Use when external series resistors are preferred, or when the output load is close enough that reflection damping is not required.

Pins:
  A1: Input 1.
  Y1: Buffered output 1.
  A2: Input 2.
  Y2: Buffered output 2.
  A3: Input 3.
  Y3: Buffered output 3.
  A4: Input 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Y4: Buffered output 4.
  A5: Input 5.
  Y5: Buffered output 5.
  A6: Input 6.
  Y6: Buffered output 6.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  EN: Additional enable input.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

74x436 vs 74x437
The 74x437 is pin compatible with 74x436. Choose 74x436 when on chip damping is required for long line signals; use 74x437 when adding your own series resistors or when the trace length is short.
74x440:
The 74x440 is a quad non inverting tridirectional bus transceiver with open collector outputs. Two enable inputs (OE1n, OE2n) and a direction control (DIR) allow four A/B channels to pass data in either direction. Open collector outputs support wired OR bus configurations.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW). Both OE1n and OE2n must be LOW to enable.
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1 (OC output when DIR=HIGH).
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Tridirectional Transceiver
Tridirectional means the device supports not just two directions but also a high impedance (off) state, making three possible modes. With two OEn pins both must be LOW to enable data flow; either HIGH disables all outputs.
74x441:
The 74x441 is an inverting quad tridirectional bus transceiver with open collector outputs. It is identical in control to the 74x440 but inverts the data on the output side. Useful when bus polarity needs to be reversed between two circuit domains.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B (inverted); LOW = B to A (inverted).
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1 (OC, inverted from A1).
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Transceiver
By inverting the data in transit, the 74x441 allows a system that uses positive logic on one bus and negative logic on another to communicate without external inverters.
74x442:
The 74x442 is a quad non inverting tridirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. It is the tri state counterpart of the 74x440 (which is open collector). DIR controls data direction; OE1n and OE2n (both active LOW) must both be asserted to enable data flow.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1.
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x440/441/442/443/444 Family
These five chips form a matrix of inverting/non inverting and OC/tri state options. The 74x442 is the non inverting, tri state version. Choose based on whether your bus uses wired OR (OC) or totem pole (tri state) signaling, and whether data polarity needs inversion.
74x443:
The 74x443 is an inverting quad tridirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. It is identical to 74x442 except data is inverted in transit. Useful for interfacing two buses where one uses inverted polarity (e.g., active HIGH to active LOW bus conversion).

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B (inverted); LOW = B to A (inverted).
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1 (inverted from A1).
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Tri State Transceiver
The combination of inversion and tri state outputs makes the 74x443 useful for bus isolation with polarity translation. Unlike OC versions, no pull up resistors are needed, giving faster edges on long lines.
74x444:
The 74x444 is a mixed polarity quad tridirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. Two channels are non inverting and two are inverting, allowing address/data bus translations where only some lines need polarity inversion. DIR and OE1n/OE2n work the same as the rest of the 744xx transceiver family.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1.
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Mixed Polarity Transceiver
The mixed polarity configuration is useful when a bus has both inverted and non inverted lines. For example, some early CPU address buses used active LOW lower bits and active HIGH upper bits. The 74x444 handles the mix in one package.
74x445:
The 74x445 decodes a 4 bit BCD input (A-D) to one of ten active LOW outputs (Y0-Y9) with high output drive capability (80 mA sink per output). Used to directly drive incandescent displays, relays, or TTL bus lines without additional driver transistors. Invalid BCD codes (10-15) leave all outputs HIGH.

Pins:
  A: BCD input bit A (weight 1, LSB).
  B: BCD input bit B (weight 2).
  C: BCD input bit C (weight 4).
  D: BCD input bit D (weight 8, MSB).
  Y0: Output 0 (active LOW when BCD = 0000).
  Y1: Output 1 (active LOW when BCD = 0001).
  Y2: Output 2 (active LOW when BCD = 0010).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  Y3: Output 3 (active LOW when BCD = 0011).
  Y4: Output 4 (active LOW when BCD = 0100).
  Y5: Output 5 (active LOW when BCD = 0101).
  Y6: Output 6 (active LOW when BCD = 0110).
  Y7: Output 7 (active LOW when BCD = 0111).
  Y8: Output 8 (active LOW when BCD = 1000).
  Y9: Output 9 (active LOW when BCD = 1001).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

BCD to Decimal-Decoding
BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) represents digits 0-9 as 4 bit binary patterns. The 74x445 converts each BCD code to a single asserted output only one of the ten outputs is LOW at a time.
The high sink current (80 mA) means you can connect indicator lamps or relays directly to the outputs with appropriate current limiting.
74x446:
The 74x446 is a quad inverting bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. Two enable inputs (OE1n, OE2n active LOW) plus DIR control data direction. Data is inverted as it passes through a HIGH on the A side becomes a LOW on the B side. A compact 16-pin alternative to the 20-pin 74x443.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW). Both OE1n and OE2n must be LOW to enable.
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B (inverted); LOW = B to A (inverted).
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1 (inverted).
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Inverting Transceiver
The inversion is useful when interfacing TTL buses of opposite active polarity. The 74x446 is the 16-pin counterpart to the 20-pin 74x443 (same function, fewer NC pins).
74x447:
The 74x447 is a BCD to 7 segment decoder/driver with open collector outputs and a low voltage output swing suited for direct LED or LCD interfacing. It is functionally identical to the 74x247 but specified for lower output voltage. LT (lamp test) forces all segments on; RBI blanks leading zeros; BI/RBO blanks and ripple chains multiple digits.

Pins:
  B: BCD input bit B (weight 2).
  C: BCD input bit C (weight 4).
  LT: Lamp Test (active LOW). Pull LOW to light all seven segments regardless of BCD input.
  BI/RBO: Blanking Input / Ripple Blanking Output. When pulled LOW externally, blanks all outputs. Goes LOW automatically when RBI suppresses a zero.
  RBI: Ripple Blanking Input (active LOW). Pull LOW to suppress a zero digit (output blanked when BCD = 0000 and RBI = LOW).
  D: BCD input bit D (weight 8, MSB).
  A: BCD input bit A (weight 1, LSB).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  e: Segment e output (active LOW, OC).
  d: Segment d output (active LOW, OC).
  c: Segment c output (active LOW, OC).
  b: Segment b output (active LOW, OC).
  a: Segment a output (active LOW, OC).
  g: Segment g output (active LOW, OC).
  f: Segment f output (active LOW, OC).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

7 Segment Display Layout
Segments a-g correspond to: a=top, b=upper right, c=lower right, d=bottom, e=lower left, f=upper left, g=middle bar. Each active LOW OC output needs a pull up resistor matched to the LED forward voltage and desired current.
74x448:
The 74x448 is a quad mixed polarity tridirectional bus transceiver with open collector outputs. Some channels pass data non inverted and some are inverted, making it suitable for buses that mix active HIGH and active LOW lines. OC outputs require external pull ups.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1.
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Mixed Polarity OC Transceiver
The combination of OC outputs and mixed polarity channels allows the 74x448 to be used on buses with wired AND logic across mixed active-level lines. Add 1 kΩ pull ups to VCC on all B outputs.
74x449:
The 74x449 is a quad non inverting bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs in a 16-pin package. DIR controls data direction; OE1n and OE2n (both active LOW) must be asserted together. It is the compact version of the 74x442 with fewer NC pins.

Pins:
  OE1n: Output Enable 1 (active LOW).
  OE2n: Output Enable 2 (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A to B; LOW = B to A.
  A1: Side A pin 1.
  B1: Side B pin 1.
  A2: Side A pin 2.
  B2: Side B pin 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  B3: Side B pin 3.
  A3: Side A pin 3.
  B4: Side B pin 4.
  A4: Side A pin 4.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Compact Non Inverting Transceiver
The 74x449 provides the same function as 74x442 in a smaller 16-pin package by eliminating the NC pins. Functionally identical; choose based on board size and routing constraints.
74x450:
The 74x450 is a 16-to-1 multiplexer with a 4 bit select (A-D), 16 data inputs (E0-E15), an active LOW global enable (Gn), and a true output (W). With 4 select lines it can address any of 16 inputs (2^4 = 16). This is the largest single package MUX in the 74xx family.

Pins:
  E0: Data input 0.
  E1: Data input 1.
  E2: Data input 2.
  E3: Data input 3.
  E4: Data input 4.
  E5: Data input 5.
  E6: Data input 6.
  E7: Data input 7.
  E8: Data input 8.
  E9: Data input 9.
  E10: Data input 10.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  E11: Data input 11.
  E12: Data input 12.
  E13: Data input 13.
  E14: Data input 14.
  E15: Data input 15.
  A: Select bit A (LSB).
  B: Select bit B.
  C: Select bit C.
  D: Select bit D (MSB).
  Gn: Global Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, W is forced LOW.
  W: MUX output. Reflects the selected input.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

16-to-1 Selection
The four select bits ABCD encode a binary number 0-15 that selects the corresponding E input. With Gn=LOW the selected E value passes to W.
Practical use: connect 16 signal sources to E0-E15 and sweep ABCD with a binary counter to read each source in sequence serial scan of 16 parallel inputs.
74x451:
The 74x451 contains two 8-to-1 multiplexers in a 24-pin package. Both sections share the lower 4 data inputs (D0-D3) and all three select lines (S0-S2), but each section has its own upper 4 data inputs (1D4-1D7, 2D4-2D7), enable (1Gn/2Gn), and complementary outputs (1W/1Wn, 2W/2Wn).

Pins:
  D0: Shared data input 0 (used by both sections).
  D1: Shared data input 1.
  D2: Shared data input 2.
  D3: Shared data input 3.
  S0: Select bit 0 (LSB). Shared by both sections.
  S1: Select bit 1. Shared.
  S2: Select bit 2 (MSB). Shared.
  1Gn: Enable for section 1 (active LOW). When HIGH, 1W is forced LOW.
  1W: MUX output of section 1 (selected input, true).
  1Wn: MUX output of section 1 (selected input, complemented).
  1D4: Private data input 4 of section 1.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  1D5: Private data input 5 of section 1.
  1D6: Private data input 6 of section 1.
  1D7: Private data input 7 of section 1.
  2D4: Private data input 4 of section 2.
  2D5: Private data input 5 of section 2.
  2D6: Private data input 6 of section 2.
  2D7: Private data input 7 of section 2.
  2Gn: Enable for section 2 (active LOW).
  2W: MUX output of section 2 (selected input, true).
  2Wn: MUX output of section 2 (selected input, complemented)
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Shared vs Private Inputs
D0-D3 are common to both sections, reducing pin count for the lower half of the address space. D4-D7 are separate per section for the upper half. This architecture is useful for dual channel signal routing where both channels share a common input pool.
74x452:
The 74x452 contains two independent synchronous BCD decade counters (0-9), each with its own clock, active LOW clear, and terminal count (TC) output for cascading. Unlike the asynchronous 74x390, both flip flops in each counter are clocked simultaneously, eliminating ripple delay between bits.

Pins:
  CLK1: Clock for counter 1 (rising edge counts).
  CLRn1: Clear for counter 1 (active LOW). Synchronous or asynchronous depending on variant.
  QA1: Output A of counter 1 (bit 0).
  QB1: Output B of counter 1.
  QC1: Output C of counter 1.
  QD1: Output D of counter 1 (MSB).
  TC1: Terminal Count of counter 1. Goes HIGH when count reaches 9.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  TC2: Terminal Count of counter 2.
  QD2: Output D of counter 2.
  QC2: Output C of counter 2.
  QB2: Output B of counter 2.
  QA2: Output A of counter 2.
  CLRn2: Clear for counter 2 (active LOW).
  CLK2: Clock for counter 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Synchronous vs Asynchronous Decade Counter
In a synchronous counter all flip flops share the clock, so all outputs change simultaneously. In an asynchronous (ripple) counter each stage clocks from the previous output, causing small delays that can produce glitches on combinational decode logic.
The TC output (HIGH at count 9) can clock the next counter for multi digit cascading.
74x453:
The 74x453 contains four independent 4-to-1 multiplexers sharing a common 2 bit select (S0, S1). Each section has four data inputs (C_0 to C_3) and one output (Y). All four selectors are steered to the same address simultaneously, making this ideal for 4-wide bus selection or function generation.

Pins:
  S0: Select bit 0 (LSB). Shared by all four sections.
  S1: Select bit 1 (MSB). Shared by all four sections.
  Y1: MUX output of section 1 (selected input).
  C1_0: Section 1 data input 0.
  C1_1: Section 1 data input 1.
  C1_2: Section 1 data input 2.
  C1_3: Section 1 data input 3.
  Y2: MUX output of section 2 (selected input).
  C2_0: Section 2 data input 0.
  C2_1: Section 2 data input 1.
  C2_2: Section 2 data input 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  C2_3: Section 2 data input 3.
  Y3: MUX output of section 3 (selected input).
  C3_0: Section 3 data input 0.
  C3_1: Section 3 data input 1.
  C3_2: Section 3 data input 2.
  C3_3: Section 3 data input 3.
  Y4: MUX output of section 4 (selected input)
  C4_0: Section 4 data input 0.
  C4_1: Section 4 data input 1.
  C4_2: Section 4 data input 2.
  C4_3: Section 4 data input 3.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

4-Wide Bus Selection
By treating the four sections as a nibble wide MUX, you can route one of four 4 bit buses to the output with a single 2 bit address. Connect S0/S1 to address bits; the four Y outputs give the selected nibble.
74x454:
The 74x454 is a dual synchronous BCD up/down decade counter with parallel preset. A common clock, up/down select (U_Dn), enable inputs (ENP, ENT), parallel load (LOADn), and clear (CLRn) are provided per counter. TCU fires at count 9 going up; TCD fires at count 0 going down.

Pins:
  CLK: Clock for counter 1 (rising edge).
  U_Dn: Direction. HIGH = count up; LOW = count down.
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel (counter 1). Both ENP and ENT must be HIGH to count.
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle. Drives TCU/TCD outputs and cascades.
  LOADn: Parallel Load (active LOW). On rising CLK, loads P0-P3.
  CLRn: Clear (active LOW). Asynchronously resets counter 1 to 0.
  P0: Preset data bit 0 for counter 1.
  P1: Preset data bit 1.
  P2: Preset data bit 2.
  P3: Preset data bit 3.
  Q0: Output bit 0 of counter 1.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q1: Output bit 1.
  Q2: Output bit 2.
  Q3: Output bit 3.
  TCD: Terminal Count Down. HIGH when counter reaches 0 going down.
  TCU: Terminal Count Up. HIGH when counter reaches 9 going up.
  CLK2: Clock for counter 2.
  ENP2: Count Enable Parallel for counter 2.
  P4: Preset data bit 0 for counter 2.
  P5: Preset data bit 1 for counter 2.
  P6: Preset data bit 2 for counter 2.
  P7: Preset data bit 3 for counter 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Up/Down Counter Cascading
Connect TCU of the lower counter to ENT of the next for up counting cascade; connect TCD similarly for down counting. ENP should be tied HIGH for cascaded sections since they should always count when the upstream counter reaches its terminal.
74x455:
The 74x455 is an octal inverting buffer/line driver with integrated even/odd parity generation. Each of the eight inputs passes through an inverter to the Y output; simultaneously the XOR tree accumulates a parity bit. EP (Even Parity) selects whether the PERR output flags even or odd parity. OEn tri states all Y outputs.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, Y0-Y7 are high impedance.
  A0: Data input 0.
  A1: Data input 1.
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5.
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  EP: Even Parity select. HIGH = even parity mode.
  PERR: Parity Error output. Flags a parity violation.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Y7: Inverted output 7.
  Y6: Inverted output 6.
  Y5: Inverted output 5.
  Y4: Inverted output 4.
  Y3: Inverted output 3.
  Y2: Inverted output 2.
  Y1: Inverted output 1.
  Y0: Inverted output 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Parity Generation
Parity is a simple error detection scheme: an extra bit is added to a data word so the total number of 1 bits is always even (even parity) or always odd (odd parity). The receiver recounts and flags a PERR if the parity is wrong.
The 74x455 generates parity on the fly as data is buffered, adding error detection without extra logic.
74x456:
The 74x456 is identical to the 74x455 but with non inverting outputs. Eight inputs pass through buffers unchanged to Y0-Y7 while the parity tree checks or generates even/odd parity. OEn tri states all Y outputs.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Data input 0.
  A1: Data input 1.
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5.
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  EP: Even Parity select.
  PERR: Parity Error output.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Y7: Buffered output 7 (non inverted).
  Y6: Buffered output 6.
  Y5: Buffered output 5.
  Y4: Buffered output 4.
  Y3: Buffered output 3.
  Y2: Buffered output 2.
  Y1: Buffered output 1.
  Y0: Buffered output 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x455 vs 74x456
The 74x455 inverts each data bit at the output; the 74x456 does not. Both calculate parity on the same input data. Use 74x456 when you need the original data on the bus with in line parity checking.
74x458:
The 74x458 computes the nines complement of a BCD digit. Given a BCD input A (0-9), it outputs 9-A. When the ZEn (Zero Element) input is asserted, the output is forced to zero. Used in BCD subtraction circuits where complement and add is used instead of direct subtraction.

Pins:
  A0: BCD input bit 0 (LSB).
  A1: BCD input bit 1.
  A2: BCD input bit 2.
  A3: BCD input bit 3 (MSB).
  ZEn: Zero Element enable. When asserted, forces output to 0.
  Y0: Nines complement output bit 0.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  Y1: Nines complement output bit 1.
  Y2: Nines complement output bit 2.
  Y3: Nines complement output bit 3.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

Nines Complement and BCD Subtraction
To subtract two BCD digits: compute the nines complement of the subtrahend, add 1 (tens complement), then add to the minuend using a BCD adder. The 74x458 handles the first step automatically.
Example: 7 − 3 = 7 + (9-3+1) = 7 + 7 = 14 → carry=1, result=4.
74x460:
The 74x460 is a 10 bit identity comparator. It compares two 10 bit words (A0-A9 vs B0-B9) bit by bit and asserts the single AEQB output when all ten pairs match. An output enable (OEn) tri states the AEQB output. Wider than the 4 bit 74x85, it is useful for address match detection in 10 bit address spaces.

Pins:
  A0: Comparator A bit 0.
  A1: Comparator A bit 1.
  A2: Comparator A bit 2.
  A3: Comparator A bit 3.
  A4: Comparator A bit 4.
  A5: Comparator A bit 5.
  A6: Comparator A bit 6.
  A7: Comparator A bit 7.
  A8: Comparator A bit 8.
  A9: Comparator A bit 9 (MSB).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, AEQB is tri stated.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  AEQB: A equals B output. HIGH when A[9:0] = B[9:0].
  B0: Comparator B bit 0.
  B1: Comparator B bit 1.
  B2: Comparator B bit 2.
  B3: Comparator B bit 3.
  B4: Comparator B bit 4.
  B5: Comparator B bit 5.
  B6: Comparator B bit 6.
  B7: Comparator B bit 7.
  B8: Comparator B bit 8.
  B9: Comparator B bit 9.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

10 bit Address Match Detector
The AEQB output is a single flag this chip does not provide magnitude comparison (A>B, A<B). For magnitude comparison, use 74x85 or cascade multiple 74x85s.
Typical use: connect A0-A9 to the CPU address bus and B0-B9 to switch selectable address constants. AEQB goes HIGH to select a memory region.
74x461:
The 74x461 is an 8 bit presettable synchronous binary counter with tri state outputs and ripple carry output (RCO). LOADn (active LOW) parallel loads P0-P7; CLRn resets asynchronously; ENP and ENT enable counting; RCO goes HIGH when the counter overflows from 255 to 0, allowing cascading.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, Q1-Q7 are tri stated.
  P0: Preset data bit 0.
  P1: Preset data bit 1.
  P2: Preset data bit 2.
  P3: Preset data bit 3.
  P4: Preset data bit 4.
  P5: Preset data bit 5.
  P6: Preset data bit 6.
  P7: Preset data bit 7.
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel. Must be HIGH with ENT to count.
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle. Must be HIGH with ENP to count. Also drives RCO.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output. HIGH when count=255 and ENT is HIGH.
  LOADn: Parallel Load (active LOW). On rising CLK loads P0-P7.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  CLRn: Asynchronous Clear (active LOW).
  Q7: Output bit 7 (MSB).
  Q6: Output bit 6.
  Q5: Output bit 5.
  Q4: Output bit 4.
  Q3: Output bit 3.
  Q2: Output bit 2.
  Q1: Output bit 1.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

8 bit Counter Cascading
Connect RCO of one 74x461 to ENT of the next for 16 bit, 24 bit, or wider counters. The ENP of each downstream stage should be connected to the RCO of the upstream stage; ENT should be a separate cascaded carry chain.
The parallel load allows arbitrary starting points useful for event timers that count down from a preset value to zero.
74x465:
The 74x465 is an octal non inverting tri state buffer with two active LOW enables (G1n, G2n) that must both be LOW to enable the outputs. When either is HIGH all eight Y outputs are high impedance. Having two enables allows the chip to be gated by two independent control signals without external NAND logic.

Pins:
  G1n: Enable 1 (active LOW). When HIGH, all outputs tri stated.
  A1: Data input 1.
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5.
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  A8: Data input 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y8: Tri state output 8.
  Y7: Tri state output 7.
  Y6: Tri state output 6.
  Y5: Tri state output 5.
  Y4: Tri state output 4.
  Y3: Tri state output 3.
  Y2: Tri state output 2.
  Y1: Tri state output 1.
  G2n: Enable 2 (active LOW). Both G1n and G2n must be LOW to enable.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Dual Enable Bus Gating
The two enable structure implements a wired AND in the enable path. You can use G1n for chip select and G2n for read/write direction without needing an external AND gate.
74x466:
