The 74x466 is an octal inverting tri state buffer with two active LOW enables (G1n, G2n). Both enables must be LOW to drive the Y outputs; each output is the complement of its input. Use when an inverting buffer with a dual enable AND condition is needed.

Pins:
  G1n: Enable 1 (active LOW).
  A1: Data input 1.
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5.
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  A8: Data input 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y8: Inverted tri state output 8.
  Y7: Inverted tri state output 7.
  Y6: Inverted tri state output 6.
  Y5: Inverted tri state output 5.
  Y4: Inverted tri state output 4.
  Y3: Inverted tri state output 3.
  Y2: Inverted tri state output 2.
  Y1: Inverted tri state output 1.
  G2n: Enable 2 (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x465 vs 74x466
Same pinout, same dual enable structure. 74x465 passes data unchanged; 74x466 inverts it. Select based on the bus polarity you need on the output side.
74x467:
The 74x467 is an octal non inverting tri state buffer with independent enables for each 4 bit nibble. G1n controls A1-A4/Y1-Y4 and G2n controls A5-A8/Y5-Y8. This split enable design allows one byte to be driven as two independently gated nibbles on the same bus.

Pins:
  G1n: Enable for lower nibble A1-A4 (active LOW).
  A1: Data input 1 (lower nibble).
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5 (upper nibble).
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  A8: Data input 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y8: Tri state output 8.
  Y7: Tri state output 7.
  Y6: Tri state output 6.
  Y5: Tri state output 5.
  Y4: Tri state output 4.
  Y3: Tri state output 3.
  Y2: Tri state output 2.
  Y1: Tri state output 1.
  G2n: Enable for upper nibble A5-A8 (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Split Nibble Enable
The 74x467 differs from the 74x465 in that G1n and G2n are independent per nibble enables rather than a shared AND pair. Use it when you need to selectively pass only the lower or upper byte of an 8 bit bus.
74x468:
The 74x468 is an octal inverting tri state buffer with split per nibble enables (G1n for A1-A4, G2n for A5-A8). Each output is the complement of its input. Combines the inverting capability of 74x466 with the split enable flexibility of 74x467.

Pins:
  G1n: Enable for lower nibble A1-A4 (active LOW).
  A1: Data input 1 (lower nibble).
  A2: Data input 2.
  A3: Data input 3.
  A4: Data input 4.
  A5: Data input 5 (upper nibble).
  A6: Data input 6.
  A7: Data input 7.
  A8: Data input 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y8: Inverted tri state output 8.
  Y7: Inverted tri state output 7.
  Y6: Inverted tri state output 6.
  Y5: Inverted tri state output 5.
  Y4: Inverted tri state output 4.
  Y3: Inverted tri state output 3.
  Y2: Inverted tri state output 2.
  Y1: Inverted tri state output 1.
  G2n: Enable for upper nibble A5-A8 (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Split Enable Buffer
Use the 74x468 when you need to selectively invert either the lower or upper nibble of an 8 bit bus independently. Both nibbles or only one can be driven at a time.
74x469:
The 74x469 is an 8 bit synchronous up/down counter with parallel load, hold enable (ENn), tri state outputs (OEn), and ripple carry output (RCO). U_Dn selects counting direction. LOADn (active LOW) parallel loads P0-P7 on the next clock. ENn inhibits counting without clearing the count. RCO signals overflow (up) or underflow (down).

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, Q0-Q7 are tri stated.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  LOADn: Parallel Load (active LOW). Loads P0-P7 on rising CLK.
  U_Dn: Up/Down. HIGH = count up; LOW = count down.
  ENn: Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, counter holds current value.
  P0: Preset input bit 0.
  P1: Preset input bit 1.
  P2: Preset input bit 2.
  P3: Preset input bit 3.
  P4: Preset input bit 4.
  P5: Preset input bit 5.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  P6: Preset input bit 6.
  P7: Preset input bit 7.
  Q0: Counter output bit 0.
  Q1: Counter output bit 1.
  Q2: Counter output bit 2.
  Q3: Counter output bit 3.
  Q4: Counter output bit 4.
  Q5: Counter output bit 5.
  Q6: Counter output bit 6.
  Q7: Counter output bit 7 (MSB).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Out. HIGH on overflow (up) or underflow (down).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Up/Down Counter with Preset
Load any start value via P0-P7 using LOADn, then count up or down. RCO provides the cascade signal for chaining multiple chips to build 16 bit or wider up/down counters.
The hold mode (ENn=HIGH) is useful for pausing the count without losing the current value, for example during a bus read operation.
74x480:
The 74x480 is a single burst error recovery circuit used in data storage and communications to correct single burst errors in serial data streams. It implements a specialized polynomial based correction algorithm. Due to its analog and complex digital nature, it is represented as a logic stub in the simulator.

Pins:
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  CLK: Clock.
  LOADn: Load (active LOW).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  ERR: Error flag output.
  Q0: Corrected data output bit 0.
  Q1: Corrected data output bit 1.
  Q2: Corrected data output bit 2.
  Q3: Corrected data output bit 3.
  Q4: Corrected data output bit 4.
  Q5: Corrected data output bit 5.
  Q6: Corrected data output bit 6.
  Q7: Corrected data output bit 7.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Burst Error Correction
A burst error is a sequence of consecutive corrupted bits in a data stream. Unlike random errors, burst errors typically arise from physical media defects, impulse noise, or synchronization loss. This chip applies a hardware polynomial algorithm to detect and correct such bursts.
Note: This device is simulated as a stub. Real burst error correction requires analog signal integrity analysis beyond the scope of logic simulation.
74x482:
The 74x482 is a 4 bit expandable pipeline control element used to build microcode sequencers and microprogrammed controllers. Multiple slices cascade to control wider instruction pipelines. Represented as a logic stub in the simulator due to its complex internal state machine.

Pins:
  I0: Instruction/control input bit 0.
  I1: Instruction/control input bit 1.
  I2: Instruction/control input bit 2.
  I3: Instruction/control input bit 3.
  I4: Instruction/control input bit 4.
  I5: Instruction/control input bit 5.
  I6: Instruction/control input bit 6.
  I7: Instruction/control input bit 7.
  I8: Instruction/control input bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y0: Control output bit 0.
  Y1: Control output bit 1.
  Y2: Control output bit 2.
  Y3: Control output bit 3.
  Y4: Control output bit 4.
  Y5: Control output bit 5.
  Y6: Control output bit 6.
  Y7: Control output bit 7.
  Y8: Control output bit 8.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Pipeline Control Slice
Microprogrammed control units use ROM lookup tables (microcode) to decode instruction opcodes into sequences of control signals. A control slice handles a portion of those control bits, with multiple slices cascaded for wider control words.
74x484:
The 74x484 converts a 2-digit BCD number (up to 99 decimal) to a 7 bit pure binary output. Two BCD digits (units D1 and tens D2, each A-D) are presented in parallel; the chip combinationally converts the BCD encoded value to its binary equivalent on Y0-Y6. OEn tri states the outputs.

Pins:
  C1: BCD units digit input bit C (weight 4).
  B1: BCD units digit input bit B (weight 2).
  A1: BCD units digit input bit A (weight 1, LSB).
  D1: BCD units digit input bit D (weight 8, MSB).
  C2: BCD tens digit input bit C.
  B2: BCD tens digit input bit B.
  A2: BCD tens digit input bit A.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  D2: BCD tens digit input bit D.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y0: Binary output bit 0 (LSB).
  Y1: Binary output bit 1.
  Y2: Binary output bit 2.
  Y3: Binary output bit 3.
  Y4: Binary output bit 4.
  Y5: Binary output bit 5.
  Y6: Binary output bit 6 (MSB, range 0-99 = 0-64h).
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

BCD to Binary Conversion
BCD represents each decimal digit separately as 4 bits. Pure binary encodes the entire value in a single binary word. For example, decimal 47 is 0100 0111 in BCD but 0101111 (47) in binary.
The 74x484 handles values 0-99, producing a 7 bit binary result (0-127 range, but only 0-99 are valid BCD inputs).
74x485:
The 74x485 is the inverse of the 74x484: it converts a 7 bit binary number to a 2-digit BCD output. Given a binary value 0-99 on I0-I6, it outputs the tens digit on D2/C2/B2/A2 and the units digit on D1/C1/B1/A1. OEn tri states all outputs.

Pins:
  I0: Binary input bit 0 (LSB).
  I1: Binary input bit 1.
  I2: Binary input bit 2.
  I3: Binary input bit 3.
  I4: Binary input bit 4.
  I5: Binary input bit 5.
  I6: Binary input bit 6 (MSB).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  NC1: No connect.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  A1: Units digit output bit A (weight 1).
  B1: Units digit output bit B (weight 2).
  C1: Units digit output bit C (weight 4).
  D1: Units digit output bit D (weight 8).
  A2: Tens digit output bit A.
  B2: Tens digit output bit B.
  C2: Tens digit output bit C.
  D2: Tens digit output bit D.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Binary to BCD Conversion
Connecting 74x485 output to a BCD 7 segment decoder (like 74x447) allows direct display of a binary counter value in decimal digits. The chain is: binary counter → 74x485 → two 74x447s → two 7 segment displays.
74x490:
The 74x490 contains two independent BCD decade counters with asynchronous (active HIGH) clear. Each counter has a divide by-2 clock (CKA) and a divide by-5 clock (CKB). Connect CKB to QA for a 0-9 BCD counter. This is functionally equivalent to the 74x390 with slight pin ordering differences.

Pins:
  CKA1: Clock A (÷2 stage) for counter 1. Falls on negative edge.
  CLR1: Clear for counter 1 (active HIGH). Asynchronously resets to 0.
  CKB1: Clock B (÷5 stage) for counter 1. Connect to QA1 for BCD.
  QA1: Output A (bit 0) of counter 1.
  QB1: Output B of counter 1.
  QC1: Output C of counter 1.
  QD1: Output D (MSB) of counter 1.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  QD2: Output D of counter 2.
  QC2: Output C of counter 2.
  QB2: Output B of counter 2.
  QA2: Output A of counter 2.
  CKB2: Clock B for counter 2.
  CLR2: Clear for counter 2 (active HIGH).
  CKA2: Clock A for counter 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

BCD Decade Counter
Connect CKB to QA to form a full BCD 0-9 counter. The ÷2 stage clocks on CKA; the ÷5 stage clocks from QA. When the count reaches 9 the next CKA pulse wraps back to 0.
74x491:
The 74x491 is a 10 bit synchronous binary up/down counter with parallel preset (P0-P9), direction select (U_Dn), enable (ENn), clear (CLRn), parallel load (LOADn), and tri state outputs (OEn). Its 10 bit width (count 0-1023) makes it useful for address generation in 1K memory systems.

Pins:
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  U_Dn: Direction. HIGH = up; LOW = down.
  ENn: Count Enable (active LOW). When HIGH, counter holds.
  CLRn: Asynchronous Clear (active LOW).
  LOADn: Parallel Load (active LOW). Loads P0-P9 on next CLK.
  P0: Preset bit 0.
  P1: Preset bit 1.
  P2: Preset bit 2.
  P3: Preset bit 3.
  P4: Preset bit 4.
  P5: Preset bit 5.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  P6: Preset bit 6.
  P7: Preset bit 7.
  P8: Preset bit 8.
  P9: Preset bit 9.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  Q0: Counter output bit 0.
  Q1: Counter output bit 1.
  Q2: Counter output bit 2.
  Q3: Counter output bit 3.
  Q4: Counter output bit 4.
  Q5: Counter output bit 5.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

10 bit Address Counter
A 10 bit counter can directly address 1024 (1K) memory locations (0x000-0x3FF). Load an arbitrary start address via P0-P9 for non zero starting DMA or memory scan operations.
74x498:
The 74x498 is an 8 bit bidirectional shift register with parallel load, tri state outputs, and independent left/right serial inputs (SRL, SRR). Two mode select bits (S0, S1) choose from: hold (00), shift right (01), shift left (10), or parallel load (11). OEn tri states Q0-Q7.

Pins:
  S0: Mode select bit 0. See mode table.
  S1: Mode select bit 1.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  SRL: Serial input for shift left mode. Data enters at Q7.
  SRR: Serial input for shift right mode. Data enters at Q0.
  P0: Parallel data input bit 0.
  P1: Parallel data input bit 1.
  P2: Parallel data input bit 2.
  P3: Parallel data input bit 3.
  P4: Parallel data input bit 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  P5: Parallel data input bit 5.
  P6: Parallel data input bit 6.
  P7: Parallel data input bit 7.
  Q0: Output bit 0.
  Q1: Output bit 1.
  Q2: Output bit 2.
  Q3: Output bit 3.
  Q4: Output bit 4.
  Q5: Output bit 5.
  Q6: Output bit 6.
  Q7: Output bit 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Mode Select Table
S1=0, S0=0: Hold (no change). S1=0, S0=1: Shift right (SRR enters at Q0). S1=1, S0=0: Shift left (SRL enters at Q7). S1=1, S0=1: Parallel load P0-P7.
74x500:
The 74x500 is a 6 bit flash (parallel) analog to digital converter. Flash ADCs compare the input voltage against 2^N reference voltages simultaneously using a resistor ladder and comparator array, making them the fastest ADC topology. 74Sim models it with a behavioral linear converter: it reads the analog VIN and VREF net voltages and drives the data pins with the corresponding digital code (see the note below).

Pins:
  VIN: Analog voltage input.
  VREF: Reference voltage input. Defines full scale range.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLK: Conversion clock.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  NC7: No connect.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  D0: Digital output bit 0 (LSB).
  D1: Digital output bit 1.
  D2: Digital output bit 2.
  D3: Digital output bit 3.
  D4: Digital output bit 4.
  D5: Digital output bit 5 (MSB).
  CC: Conversion Complete flag.
  NC8: No connect.
  NC9: No connect.
  NC10: No connect.
  NC11: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Flash ADC Architecture
A flash ADC uses 2^N-1 comparators in parallel to convert an analog input in a single clock cycle. The 6 bit version requires 63 comparators. This makes it extremely fast but power hungry and expensive compared to SAR or delta sigma ADCs.
Note: Behavioral model. The simulator reads the analog VIN and VREF net voltages and drives D0..D5 with code = round((VIN / VREF) * 63), clipped to 0..63. The conversion is treated as instantaneous (CLK is not stepped), VREF defaults to VCC if unconnected, and CC is held HIGH ("ready"). OEn=HIGH places D0..D5 in HiZ. The internal resistor-ladder/comparator array is not modelled at the transistor level.
74x505:
The 74x505 is a complete 8 bit successive approximation ADC integrating analog comparator, internal DAC, and SAR logic into one package. As a self contained converter it needs only VIN and VREF no external DAC/comparator feedback loop. 74Sim models it with a behavioral linear converter: it reads the analog VIN and VREF net voltages and drives the data pins with the corresponding digital code (see the note below).

Pins:
  VIN: Analog voltage input.
  VREF: Reference voltage.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLK: Conversion clock.
  STARTn: Start conversion (active LOW).
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  D0: Digital output bit 0 (LSB).
  D1: Digital output bit 1.
  D2: Digital output bit 2.
  D3: Digital output bit 3.
  D4: Digital output bit 4.
  D5: Digital output bit 5.
  D6: Digital output bit 6.
  D7: Digital output bit 7 (MSB).
  EOC: End Of Conversion output.
  NC7: No connect.
  NC8: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Integrated SAR ADC
The 74x505 packages the entire SAR ADC signal chain on a single chip. Apply VIN, assert STARTn, clock 8 cycles, and read D0-D7 when EOC asserts.
Note: Behavioral model. The simulator reads the analog VIN and VREF net voltages and drives D0..D7 with code = round((VIN / VREF) * 255), clipped to 0..255. The conversion is treated as instantaneous (CLK and STARTn are not stepped), VREF defaults to VCC if unconnected, and EOC is held HIGH ("ready"). OEn=HIGH places D0..D7 in HiZ. The internal DAC/comparator successive-approximation loop is not modelled cycle-by-cycle.
74x508:
The 74x508 is an 8x8 bit two's complement parallel multiplier. It multiplies signed 8 bit operands A and B combinationally, producing the lower 6 bits of the 16 bit product on P0-P5. Additional product bits require cascading. Parallel multipliers use arrays of partial product adders for maximum speed.

Pins:
  A0: Multiplicand bit 0 (LSB).
  A1: Multiplicand bit 1.
  A2: Multiplicand bit 2.
  A3: Multiplicand bit 3.
  A4: Multiplicand bit 4.
  A5: Multiplicand bit 5.
  A6: Multiplicand bit 6.
  A7: Multiplicand bit 7 (MSB / sign bit in 2s complement).
  B0: Multiplier bit 0.
  B1: Multiplier bit 1.
  B2: Multiplier bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Multiplier bit 3.
  B4: Multiplier bit 4.
  B5: Multiplier bit 5.
  B6: Multiplier bit 6.
  B7: Multiplier bit 7 (sign bit).
  P0: Product bit 0 (LSB).
  P1: Product bit 1.
  P2: Product bit 2.
  P3: Product bit 3.
  P4: Product bit 4.
  P5: Product bit 5 (MSB available on chip).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Parallel Multiplication
Multiplying two N-bit numbers produces a 2N-bit product. Two's complement encoding handles signed operands. The chip internally forms all partial products and sums them in a single combinational pass much faster than shift and add approaches.
74x515:
The 74x515 is a 2-to-4 decoder with nine programmable enable inputs (E0-E8), intended for memory address decoding. The nine enables allow very specific address patterns to be decoded without external glue logic. A0/A1 select one of four active LOW outputs; all nine enables must be satisfied simultaneously.

Pins:
  A0: Address input bit 0 (LSB).
  A1: Address input bit 1.
  E0: Enable input 0 (logic level determines active state).
  E1: Enable input 1.
  E2: Enable input 2.
  E3: Enable input 3.
  E4: Enable input 4.
  E5: Enable input 5.
  E6: Enable input 6.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  E7: Enable input 7.
  E8: Enable input 8.
  Y0n: Decoded output 0 (active LOW).
  Y1n: Decoded output 1 (active LOW).
  Y2n: Decoded output 2 (active LOW).
  Y3n: Decoded output 3 (active LOW).
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Programmable Address Decoder
With 9 enable inputs the 74x515 can decode from a wide address bus. Connect high address lines to E0-E8 to select a specific 4-location window in the memory map. Each Y output selects a specific chip-select region.
74x516:
The 74x516 is a 4x4 bit two's complement parallel multiplier that produces a full 8 bit product (P0-P7). Both signed operands A (4 bit) and B (4 bit) are multiplied combinationally. The full product is available without cascading, making this chip simpler to use than wider multipliers.

  A1: Multiplicand bit 1.
  A2: Multiplicand bit 2.
  A3: Multiplicand bit 3 (MSB/sign).
  B0: Multiplier bit 0.
  B1: Multiplier bit 1.
  B2: Multiplier bit 2.
  B3: Multiplier bit 3 (MSB/sign).
  P0: Product bit 0 (LSB).
  P1: Product bit 1.
  P2: Product bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  P3: Product bit 3.
  P4: Product bit 4.
  P5: Product bit 5.
  P6: Product bit 6.
  P7: Product bit 7 (MSB).

  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  NC6: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

4x4 Parallel Multiplier
A 4x4 multiply produces an 8 bit result. Range: -8 to +7 signed (-128 to +49 product). The chip completes the operation in a single propagation delay, no clock required.
74x518: The 74x518 is an 8 bit identity comparator with on chip 20 kΩ pull up resistors on all data inputs and an open collector EQ (active LOW) output. EQ is asserted LOW when A[7:0] equals B[7:0] and G1 (active LOW) is asserted. The built in pull ups simplify interfacing with passive bus holders or CMOS devices. Pins: G1n: Enable (active LOW). Assertion required for comparison output to respond; when negated, EQ (active LOW) is forced HIGH. A0: Data bus A, bit 0. A1: Data bus A, bit 1. A2: Data bus A, bit 2. A3: Data bus A, bit 3. A4: Data bus A, bit 4. A5: Data bus A, bit 5. A6: Data bus A, bit 6. A7: Data bus A, bit 7. GND: Ground reference (pin 10). B0: Data bus B, bit 0. B1: Data bus B, bit 1. B2: Data bus B, bit 2. B3: Data bus B, bit 3. B4: Data bus B, bit 4. B5: Data bus B, bit 5. B6: Data bus B, bit 6. B7: Data bus B, bit 7. EQn: EQ Equal output (active LOW, open collector). Asserted when A=B and G1 (active LOW) is asserted. VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20). Open Collector Wired AND With open collector outputs, multiple 74x518 outputs can be tied together. The combined output is LOW only when ALL comparators agree on equality useful for comparing 16- or 32 bit buses by chaining chips.
74x519: The 74x519 is an 8 bit identity comparator with an open collector EQ (active LOW) output, identical in function to the 74x518 but without the internal 20 kΩ pull up resistors. Use when the bus pull ups are provided externally or bus drive levels are different. Pins: G1n: Enable (active LOW). A0: Data bus A, bit 0. A1: Data bus A, bit 1. A2: Data bus A, bit 2. A3: Data bus A, bit 3. A4: Data bus A, bit 4. A5: Data bus A, bit 5. A6: Data bus A, bit 6. A7: Data bus A, bit 7. GND: Ground reference (pin 10). B0: Data bus B, bit 0. B1: Data bus B, bit 1. B2: Data bus B, bit 2. B3: Data bus B, bit 3. B4: Data bus B, bit 4. B5: Data bus B, bit 5. B6: Data bus B, bit 6. B7: Data bus B, bit 7. EQn: EQ Equal output (active LOW, open collector). VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20). 74x518 vs 74x519 74x518 includes on chip 20 kΩ pull ups on A and B inputs; 74x519 does not. Both have identical open collector EQn outputs. Choose based on whether the bus already has pull ups in place.
74x520:
The 74x520 is an 8 bit identity comparator with inverted (active HIGH) EQ output and on chip 20 kΩ pull up inputs. EQ goes HIGH when A equals B and G1 (active LOW) is asserted. Use when downstream logic expects a positive (active HIGH) equality signal.

Pins:
  G1n: Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Data bus A, bit 0.
  A1: Data bus A, bit 1.
  A2: Data bus A, bit 2.
  A3: Data bus A, bit 3.
  A4: Data bus A, bit 4.
  A5: Data bus A, bit 5.
  A6: Data bus A, bit 6.
  A7: Data bus A, bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B0: Data bus B, bit 0.
  B1: Data bus B, bit 1.
  B2: Data bus B, bit 2.
  B3: Data bus B, bit 3.
  B4: Data bus B, bit 4.
  B5: Data bus B, bit 5.
  B6: Data bus B, bit 6.
  B7: Data bus B, bit 7.
  EQ: Equal output (active HIGH, tri state). HIGH when A=B and G1 (active LOW) is asserted.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Output Comparator
The 74x520 and 74x521 both produce active HIGH (non inverting sense) EQ outputs unlike the 74x518/519 which produce active LOW EQn. The tri state output allows bus sharing.
74x521: The 74x521 compares two 8 bit words, A (A0-A7) and B (B0-B7), and tells you one thing: whether they are exactly equal. Its single output goes LOW when every bit of A matches the matching bit of B and the enable is LOW; any mismatch, or a disabled chip, leaves it HIGH. It tests equality only there is no greater than or less than output, which is what makes it an identity comparator rather than a full magnitude comparator. The output is totem pole, so it drives the line both ways and needs no pull up resistor, and the chip has no built in input pull ups (that is the one thing separating it from the otherwise identical 74x520). The classic job is address decoding: put the address bus on A, fix B to a chosen address, and the output drops LOW to select a device the moment the bus lands on that address. Two things catch people out both the output and the enable are active LOW, and the A and B pins are interleaved down the package (A0, B0, A1, B1, and so on) rather than grouped, so check the pinout before wiring. Pins: G1n: Enable, active LOW (pin 1). The output only responds while this pin is LOW; hold it HIGH and the output is forced HIGH no matter what A and B are. It doubles as an expansion input for chaining chips (see below). A0: Word A input, bit 0 (pin 2). B0: Word B input, bit 0 (pin 3). A1: Word A input, bit 1 (pin 4). B1: Word B input, bit 1 (pin 5). A2: Word A input, bit 2 (pin 6). B2: Word B input, bit 2 (pin 7). A3: Word A input, bit 3 (pin 8). B3: Word B input, bit 3 (pin 9). GND: Ground reference (pin 10). A4: Word A input, bit 4 (pin 11). B4: Word B input, bit 4 (pin 12). A5: Word A input, bit 5 (pin 13). B5: Word B input, bit 5 (pin 14). A6: Word A input, bit 6 (pin 15). B6: Word B input, bit 6 (pin 16). A7: Word A input, bit 7 (pin 17). B7: Word B input, bit 7 (pin 18). EQn: A=B output, active LOW (pin 19). Goes LOW only when A equals B and the enable is LOW; HIGH otherwise. Totem pole output, so it needs no pull up resistor. VCC: Positive supply (+5 V) at pin 20. How the comparison works Inside, the chip compares A and B one bit at a time: A0 against B0, A1 against B1, and so on up to A7 against B7. A pair "agrees" when both bits are the same, both HIGH or both LOW. Only when all eight pairs agree does the chip call the two words equal. The result appears on a single output, and it is active LOW: a match pulls the output down to LOW (0 V), while any mismatch leaves it HIGH. This is the opposite of what many people expect, so it is worth saying twice: LOW means equal. The enable gates the whole thing. While the enable is LOW the output reflects the comparison. Take the enable HIGH and the output is forced HIGH regardless of A and B, which lets you switch a comparator off or chain several of them (more on that below). output is LOW only when enable = LOW AND A0..A7 equals B0..B7 enable=LOW, A equals B -> output = LOW enable=LOW, A not equal B -> output = HIGH enable=HIGH, anything -> output = HIGH Address decoding and cascading The most common job for a 74x521 is spotting when an address bus points at one particular device. Wire the address lines to A0-A7, set B0-B7 to the address you want to catch (with pull up and pull down resistors or a DIP switch), and when the bus matches your fixed pattern the output goes LOW. That LOW can drive a chip-select input directly, since chip-selects are almost always active LOW too. The enable doubles as an expansion input, which is how you compare words wider than 8 bits. Chain chips by feeding one chip’s output into the next chip’s enable: the second chip only reports a match when its own eight bits agree AND the first chip already saw its eight bits agree. Two 74x521s in a row compare 16 bits, three compare 24, and so on. Tie the first chip’s enable LOW to start the chain. Common uses - Address decoding and chip-select generation in CPU and memory systems. - Matching an address or data bus against a value set on a DIP switch. - Checking whether two 8 bit buses are carrying the same value. - Comparing words wider than 8 bits by chaining chips through the enable/expansion input. Gotchas - The output is active LOW. A match gives you LOW, not HIGH the opposite of what most people expect. - The enable is active LOW too. Tie it to ground to keep the comparison live; leave it HIGH and the output sits HIGH forever. - The A and B pins interleave down the package: A0=2, B0=3, A1=4, B1=5, and after GND, A4=11, B4=12, and so on. They are not two tidy groups, and this is the easiest place to miswire the chip. - It reports equality only there is no greater than or less than output. For magnitude comparison use a full comparator such as the 4 bit 74x85, or the 74x682/74x684 family (which add a P>Q output). - The 74x521 has NO input pull up resistors; the otherwise identical 74x520 adds 20 kOhm pull ups on the inputs. It is also functionally identical to the 74x688.
74x522:
The 74x522 combines the inverting (active HIGH EQ) output style, on chip 20 kΩ input pull ups, and an open collector output. With open collector, multiple 74x522s can be wired together: EQ is HIGH on the wired OR bus only when any one comparator sees equality (when G1 is asserted and A equals B).

  G1n: Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Data bus A, bit 0.
  A1: Data bus A, bit 1.
  A2: Data bus A, bit 2.
  A3: Data bus A, bit 3.
  A4: Data bus A, bit 4.
  A5: Data bus A, bit 5.
  A6: Data bus A, bit 6.
  A7: Data bus A, bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B0: Data bus B, bit 0.
  B1: Data bus B, bit 1.
  B2: Data bus B, bit 2.
  B3: Data bus B, bit 3.
  B4: Data bus B, bit 4.
  B5: Data bus B, bit 5.
  B6: Data bus B, bit 6.
  B7: Data bus B, bit 7.
  EQ: Equal output (active HIGH, open collector). HIGH (via pull up) when A=B and G1 (active LOW) is asserted.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

OC Wired OR Match
Connect multiple 74x522 EQ outputs together with a single pull up resistor to form a priority or multi source match detector any comparator asserting equality pulls the bus HIGH.
74x524: The 74x524 is an 8 bit registered identity comparator: the A inputs are clocked into internal flip flops on the rising CLK edge, then continuously compared with the live B inputs. EQ (active LOW) is asserted when the stored A value matches B and G1 (active LOW) is asserted. This allows comparing a stored reference value against a changing bus without external registers. Pins: CLK: Clock (rising edge). Latches A[7:0] into internal register. A0: Data bus A, bit 0 (registered on CLK). A1: Data bus A, bit 1. A2: Data bus A, bit 2. A3: Data bus A, bit 3. A4: Data bus A, bit 4. A5: Data bus A, bit 5. A6: Data bus A, bit 6. A7: Data bus A, bit 7. GND: Ground reference (pin 10). B0: Data bus B, bit 0 (live comparison input). B1: Data bus B, bit 1. B2: Data bus B, bit 2. B3: Data bus B, bit 3. B4: Data bus B, bit 4. B5: Data bus B, bit 5. B6: Data bus B, bit 6. B7: Data bus B, bit 7. EQn: EQ Equal output (active LOW, open collector). VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20). Registered Reference Comparator Load a reference value onto the A bus and clock it in once. Then the chip continuously reports whether the B bus equals that stored reference without needing an external register chip.
74x531:
The 74x531 is an octal transparent latch with tri state outputs. When LE (Latch Enable) is HIGH, outputs follow inputs (transparent mode). When LE goes LOW, inputs are captured and held. OEn independently controls output drive. This is the non inverting variant of the 74x533/535 family.

Transparent Latch vs Edge Triggered Register
A transparent latch is level sensitive: while LE is HIGH, data passes through. An edge triggered register (74x532/536) captures data only on the clock edge. Latches are more susceptible to glitches during the enable phase.
74x532:
The 74x532 is an octal D type edge triggered register (8 flip flops) with tri state outputs. All 8 data inputs are clocked on the shared CLK rising edge. OEn tri states the outputs for bus sharing. This is the non inverting counterpart to 74x534.

  D1: Data input 1.

  D3: Data input 3.

  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.

Octal Bus Register
The 74x532 captures an 8 bit bus snapshot on each clock edge. It is commonly used as a pipeline register, address latch, or I/O port latch in microprocessor bus interfaces.
74x533:
The 74x533 is an octal transparent latch with inverting (active LOW) outputs. When LE is HIGH outputs follow inverted inputs; when LE goes LOW data is held. OEn tri states all outputs. Equivalent to 74x531 with a built in inversion stage on each output.

74x533 vs 74x535
Both are inverting octal latches with tri state outputs. They are functionally identical choose based on package or availability.

The 74x534 is an octal D type edge triggered register with inverting outputs. Data is captured on the rising CLK edge; each Q output presents the complement of the captured D input. OEn tri states all outputs. The inverting output is useful when subsequent logic needs an active LOW data bus.

74x532 vs 74x534
74x532 has non inverting outputs (Q = captured D); 74x534 has inverting outputs (Q = complement of captured D). Same clock and OE structure.

The 74x535 is an octal transparent latch with inverting tri state outputs, functionally equivalent to the 74x533. LE HIGH = transparent (outputs = /D); LE LOW = hold. OEn tri states the outputs.

Inverting Transparent Latch
Use for bus inversion with hold capability. When LE is HIGH, outputs are continuously /D; pulling LE LOW freezes the inverted data regardless of subsequent D input changes.

The 74x536 is an octal D type edge triggered register with inverting outputs, functionally equivalent to the 74x534. Data captured on CLK rising edge is presented inverted at Q0n-Q7n. OEn tri states all outputs.

74x537:
The 74x537 is a 4-to-10 BCD to decimal decoder with tri state outputs. For each valid BCD input (0-9), exactly one of Y0-Y9 is driven HIGH; invalid codes (10-15) produce all outputs LOW. OEn tri states all outputs. Unlike most decoders in this family, the outputs are active HIGH.

BCD to One of Ten Decode
Drive 10 individual LEDs or relay drivers directly one per decimal digit. The tri state outputs allow multiple decoders to share an output bus, enabled by OEn.
74x538:
The 74x538 is a 3 to 8 line decoder/demultiplexer with three state outputs. Three address inputs (A, B, C) select one of eight outputs; two active LOW enables (G2A and G2B) and one active HIGH enable (G1) must all be satisfied. An additional OE (active LOW) tri states the selected output. Useful as a demux or address decoder when a third enable level is needed.

  G1: Enable 1 (active HIGH). Must be asserted for chip enable.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). When negated (HIGH), all Y outputs tri stated.

  Y4: Active LOW output 4.

  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.

3 to 8 Decoder with Tri State
Identical enable structure to the 74x138 but adds OEn to individually tri state the output bus. Useful when the Y outputs drive a shared bus where only one decoder is active at a time.

The 74x539 contains two independent 2-to-4 line decoders, each with an active LOW enable (G), an active LOW OE for tri state control, and four outputs. Both sections share the same 20-pin package. Equivalent to two 74x139 sections but with additional tri state output control.

Dual 2-to-4 Decoder
Each section independently decodes 2 address bits to 4 active LOW outputs. Use both sections to implement an 8-output decoder (using A0/A1 as lower bits and G/OEn as upper select).
74x540:
The 74x540 is an octal (8 bit) inverting buffer and line driver with 3-state outputs. Each output Y is the inverse of its matching A input, and all eight outputs share two active low enables, OE1 and OE2. Both must be LOW for the chip to drive the bus; raise either one HIGH and all eight outputs go to high impedance. Its defining feature is the pinout: every input is on one side of the package and every output on the other, so a byte wide bus flows straight across a board without traces crossing. It does the same job as the 74x240 but with this layout-friendly arrangement, and it is the inverting twin of the 74x541, which passes data through unchanged.

Pins:
  OE1: Output Enable 1 (active LOW). Must be LOW, together with OE2, for the outputs to drive. HIGH forces all eight outputs to high impedance.
  OE2: Output Enable 2 (active LOW). Same effect as OE1: HIGH forces all eight outputs to high impedance.
  A1: Data input 1. Drives output Y1.
  A2: Data input 2. Drives output Y2.
  A3: Data input 3. Drives output Y3.
  A4: Data input 4. Drives output Y4.
  A5: Data input 5. Drives output Y5.
  A6: Data input 6. Drives output Y6.
  A7: Data input 7. Drives output Y7.
  A8: Data input 8. Drives output Y8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y1: Inverted 3-state output 1: NOT(A1) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y2: Inverted 3-state output 2: NOT(A2) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y3: Inverted 3-state output 3: NOT(A3) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y4: Inverted 3-state output 4: NOT(A4) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y5: Inverted 3-state output 5: NOT(A5) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y6: Inverted 3-state output 6: NOT(A6) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y7: Inverted 3-state output 7: NOT(A7) when enabled, else high impedance.
  Y8: Inverted 3-state output 8: NOT(A8) when enabled, else high impedance.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Buffer With Two Enables
A buffer copies its input to its output. This one also inverts: each output is the opposite logic level of its matching input (Y1 tracks A1, Y2 tracks A2, and so on). The point of a buffer is not to change the logic but to redrive it. The output is a fresh, strong copy that can feed a heavily loaded bus without slowing or weakening the original signal, which is why the datasheet also calls it a line driver.
The outputs are 3-state: besides HIGH and LOW, each one has a third state, high impedance, where it is effectively disconnected so other chips can drive the same wire. Two pins control this, OE1 and OE2, and both are active LOW. Internally they feed a NOR gate, so the outputs drive only when OE1 and OE2 are both LOW. Take either one HIGH and all eight outputs release the bus at once.
Simplification: the simulator switches the outputs instantly. A real 74x540 takes a few nanoseconds for an output to follow its input, or to enter and leave high impedance.
  Y = NOT(A)   when OE1 = 0 and OE2 = 0
  Y = Hi Z     when OE1 = 1 or OE2 = 1
  A=0 → Y=1 | A=1 → Y=0   (outputs enabled)

Flow-Through Pinout
The 74x540 does the same job as the 74x240 octal inverting buffer, but its pins are arranged differently. All eight inputs (A1 to A8) run down pins 2 to 9 on one side, and all eight outputs (Y1 to Y8) run down pins 11 to 18 on the other. On a circuit board this lets a byte wide bus pass straight through the chip without traces crossing over each other. The datasheet calls this a data flow-through pinout, and it is the main reason to pick a 540 or 541 over a 240 or 244.
The enables differ too. The 74x240 splits its eight outputs into two groups of four, each with its own enable. The 540 instead gives you two enables that both act on all eight outputs together. That is useful when two conditions, say a chip-select and a read strobe, must both be true before the buffer is allowed onto the bus, with no external AND gate needed.

Common Uses
  - Driving an 8 bit address or data bus, refreshing the signal so it can fan out to many inputs.
  - Sharing a bus between several devices: each 540 stays in high impedance until its turn, then both enables go LOW and it drives.
  - Gating a byte onto a bus only when two separate control signals agree, using OE1 and OE2 as the two conditions.
  - Inverting a whole byte in one step, when the inversion is wanted rather than a nuisance.

Gotchas
  - Both enables must be LOW to drive. If you only need one, tie the unused enable to ground; leaving it HIGH keeps the entire chip in high impedance and nothing reaches the outputs.
  - The outputs invert. Forget that and every bit comes out backwards. Reach for the 74x541 when you want the data unchanged.
  - Do not let two enabled 3-state outputs drive the same wire at once. If one pushes HIGH and another LOW you get bus contention, a near short that wastes current and can overheat parts. Enable only one driver per wire at a time.
  - Inputs and outputs are numbered to match (A1 pairs with Y1) even though they sit on opposite sides of the package. Wire by pin name, not by physical position.
74x543:
The 74x543 is an octal registered transceiver that stores data before driving it in either direction. LEAB and LEBA are latch enable inputs for A-to B and B-to A paths respectively. CLK optionally registers the latch output. OEABn and OEBAn independently enable each direction. Non inverting outputs.

Pins:
  OEABn: Output Enable A-to B (active LOW).
  OEBAn: Output Enable B-to A (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = hold.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Clock for registered mode.
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Registered Transceiver
A registered transceiver stores data internally before driving the output bus. This prevents bus contention during direction reversal and allows data to be held stable while the bus changes direction.
74x544:
The 74x544 is the inverting output version of the 74x543. Same registered transceiver structure with LEAB, LEBA, CLK, DIR, OEABn, and OEBAn controls, but all data paths invert the data as it passes through the register.

Pins:
  OEABn: Output Enable A-to B (active LOW).
  OEBAn: Output Enable B-to A (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Clock.
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x543 vs 74x544
74x543 passes data non inverted; 74x544 inverts data through the register. Both have the same control structure. Use 74x544 when the receiving bus uses complemented logic.
74x545:
The 74x545 is an octal bidirectional bus transceiver. DIR selects direction (HIGH = A-to B, LOW = B-to A) and OE (active LOW) enables the drive. Outputs are non inverting. It is functionally equivalent to the 74x245 with the same simple DIR/OE interface.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Bidirectional Bus Driver
Bidirectional transceivers are essential in microprocessor data buses where the CPU must both read from and write to the same bus lines. The DIR pin determines which side is driving at any time.
Note: Never assert OE while switching DIR without a brief tri state interval to prevent bus contention.
74x546:
The 74x546 is an 8 bit bidirectional registered transceiver. It includes register stages in both A-to B and B-to A paths, gated by CLK and the LEAB/LEBA latch enables, with separate OEABn/OEBAn output enables per direction. It allows safe, registered bus data exchange in both directions.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable for A-to B path.
  LEBA: Latch Enable for B-to A path.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Clock for register capture.
  DIR: Direction select.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Registered Bidirectional Interface
The register stages in the 74x546 decouple the two bus sides, allowing each to be clocked independently. This is useful in asynchronous bus interfaces where A-side and B-side operate at different speeds.
74x547:
The 74x547 is an 8 bit bidirectional latched transceiver. It includes transparent latches (LEAB for A-to B, LEBA for B-to A) in each data path, with separate OEABn/OEBAn output enables. Unlike the 74x546, there is no separate clock; the latch operates level triggered (transparent when LE is HIGH).

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = hold.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  DIR: Direction select.
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Latched Bidirectional Transceiver
Use LEAB/LEBA to capture and hold data on either bus side before enabling the output. This prevents glitches during bus direction reversal.
74547F:
The 74x547F is a 3 to 8 decoder/demultiplexer with address latches and an ACK (acknowledge) output. STB latches the address inputs A0-A2 on the appropriate edge; G enables the decode. ACK provides handshaking feedback for bus or DMA acknowledge protocols.

Pins:
  A0: Address input bit 0.
  A1: Address input bit 1.
  A2: Address input bit 2.
  STB: Strobe (latch enable). Latches A0-A2 on assertion.
  G: Enable (active HIGH). Must be HIGH to drive outputs.
  ACK: Acknowledge output. Asserts when decode completes.
  Y7: Decoded output 7.
  Y6: Decoded output 6.
  Y5: Decoded output 5.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y4: Decoded output 4.
  Y3: Decoded output 3.
  Y2: Decoded output 2.
  Y1: Decoded output 1.
  Y0: Decoded output 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Decoder with Handshake
The ACK output signals that the chip has latched the address and driven the appropriate output. This is useful in bus protocols where the master must confirm the address was decoded before releasing the bus.
74x548:
The 74x548 is an 8 bit two stage pipelined register with separate stage clocks (CLK1, CLK2). Data is clocked from D inputs into stage 1 on CLK1, then from stage 1 to stage 2 on CLK2. The tri state output (OEn) reads stage 2. This creates a 2-cycle pipeline delay useful in high speed datapath designs.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLK1: Clock for stage 1 (D inputs to internal register).
  CLK2: Clock for stage 2 (stage 1 to Q outputs).
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q0: Pipelined output bit 0.
  Q1: Pipelined output bit 1.
  Q2: Pipelined output bit 2.
  Q3: Pipelined output bit 3.
  Q4: Pipelined output bit 4.
  Q5: Pipelined output bit 5.
  Q6: Pipelined output bit 6.
  Q7: Pipelined output bit 7.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Two Stage Pipeline
A two stage register pipeline adds a 2-cycle latency but allows each pipeline stage to be clocked at the timing of a different downstream circuit. CLK1 and CLK2 can run at different phases or frequencies.
74548F:
The 74x548F is a 3 to 8 decoder/demultiplexer with an ACK (acknowledge) output but without the address latch of the 74x547F. Enable G must be HIGH; address inputs A0-A2 immediately select one of Y0-Y7; ACK signals a completed decode for bus handshaking.

Pins:
  A0: Address input bit 0.
  A1: Address input bit 1.
  A2: Address input bit 2.
  G: Enable (active HIGH).
  ACK: Acknowledge output.
  Y7: Decoded output 7.
  Y6: Decoded output 6.
  Y5: Decoded output 5.
  Y4: Decoded output 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  Y3: Decoded output 3.
  Y2: Decoded output 2.
  Y1: Decoded output 1.
  Y0: Decoded output 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  NC4: No connect.
  NC5: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

3 to 8 with ACK
Like 74x547F but without the latching. ACK is asserted after the output has settled to confirm decode completion.
74x549:
The 74x549 is an 8 bit two stage pipelined transparent latch. LE1 controls the first latch stage and LE2 the second. OEn tri states the output. The double latch pipeline is used in bus interfaces needing two stable hold stages before the output is driven.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  LE1: Latch Enable for stage 1. HIGH = transparent; LOW = hold.
  LE2: Latch Enable for stage 2.
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q0: Pipelined output bit 0.
  Q1: Pipelined output bit 1.
  Q2: Pipelined output bit 2.
  Q3: Pipelined output bit 3.
  Q4: Pipelined output bit 4.
  Q5: Pipelined output bit 5.
  Q6: Pipelined output bit 6.
  Q7: Pipelined output bit 7.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x548 vs 74x549
74x548 is edge triggered (clock); 74x549 is level triggered (latch). Use 74x549 for gated transparent pipelines and 74x548 where clean edge triggered sampling is required.
74x559:
The 74x559 is an 8 bit expandable two's complement multiplier/divider slice. It computes a partial product using A (8 bit) and a 3 bit portion of B (B0-B2), with carry in (TCn) and carry out (TCp) for cascading. Cascade multiple chips to handle wider B operands.

Pins:
  A0: Multiplicand bit 0.
  A1: Multiplicand bit 1.
  A2: Multiplicand bit 2.
  A3: Multiplicand bit 3.
  A4: Multiplicand bit 4.
  A5: Multiplicand bit 5.
  A6: Multiplicand bit 6.
  A7: Multiplicand bit 7.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  TCn: Two's complement carry in (cascade input).
  TCp: Two's complement carry out (cascade output).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  P0: Product bit 0.
  P1: Product bit 1.
  P2: Product bit 2.
  P3: Product bit 3.
  P4: Product bit 4.
  P5: Product bit 5.
  P6: Product bit 6.
  P7: Product bit 7.
  B0: Multiplier bit 0.
  B1: Multiplier bit 1.
  B2: Multiplier bit 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Expandable Multiplier Slice
Chain multiple 74x559 chips to build a wider multiplier. TCp from the lower significance chip feeds TCn of the higher. The OEn allows the result bus to be shared.
74x560:
The 74x560 is a synchronous 4 bit decade (BCD, 0-9) counter with tri state outputs and synchronous active LOW clear. Parallel load (active HIGH LOAD), two count enables (ENP, ENT), and ripple carry output (RCO) allow full cascading. OEn tri states QA-QD.

Pins:
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW). Resets to 0 on next CLK.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel. Both ENP and ENT must be HIGH to count.
  LOAD: Parallel Load (active HIGH). Loads A-D on next CLK.
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle. Also gates RCO.
  D: Parallel data input D (weight 8).
  C: Parallel data input C (weight 4).
  B: Parallel data input B (weight 2).
  A: Parallel data input A (weight 1).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  QD: Counter output D.
  QC: Counter output C.
  QB: Counter output B.
  QA: Counter output A.
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output. HIGH when count is 9 and ENT is HIGH.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Synchronous Decade Counter
The 74x560 counts 0 through 9 in BCD. RCO goes HIGH at count 9 (when ENT is HIGH), providing the cascade carry for chaining multiple counters into multi digit counters.
74x561:
The 74x561 is a synchronous 4 bit binary (0-15) counter with tri state outputs, synchronous clear, parallel load, two count enables (ENP, ENT), and ripple carry output. It is the binary version of the 74x560, counting modulo-16 rather than modulo-10.

Pins:
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW).
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel.
  LOAD: Parallel Load (active HIGH).
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle. Also gates RCO.
  D: Parallel data input D.
  C: Parallel data input C.
  B: Parallel data input B.
  A: Parallel data input A.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  QD: Counter output D (MSB).
  QC: Counter output C.
  QB: Counter output B.
  QA: Counter output A (LSB).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output. HIGH at count 15 and ENT=HIGH.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x560 vs 74x561
74x560 counts BCD (0-9, modulo-10); 74x561 counts binary (0-15, modulo-16). Both have the same pinout, enables, and OEn. RCO cascades to ENP/ENT of the next chip in both cases.
74x563:
The 74x563 is an octal D type transparent latch with inverting outputs and tri state output control. When LE is HIGH, outputs follow inverted inputs; when LE goes LOW, data is held. OEn enables outputs. This is one of several 74x533/535/563 equivalent devices choice depends on package availability.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  LE: Latch Enable. HIGH = transparent; LOW = hold.
  Q0n: Inverted output 0.
  Q1n: Inverted output 1.
  Q2n: Inverted output 2.
  Q3n: Inverted output 3.
  Q4n: Inverted output 4.
  Q5n: Inverted output 5.
  Q6n: Inverted output 6.
  Q7n: Inverted output 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Octal Latch
The 74x563 provides bus hold latch functionality with inverted outputs. It is interchangeable with 74x533 and 74x535 in most designs.
74x564:
The 74x564 is an octal D type edge triggered flip flop with inverting outputs and tri state output control. On each rising edge of CLK, each output Qn is set to the complement of the corresponding D input. OE (active LOW) enables all outputs; negating it (HIGH) tri states them. It is the inverting complement of the 74x574.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). Tri states all Qn outputs when negated (HIGH).
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock (rising edge). Captures all D inputs simultaneously.
  Qn0: Inverted register output 0.
  Qn1: Inverted register output 1.
  Qn2: Inverted register output 2.
  Qn3: Inverted register output 3.
  Qn4: Inverted register output 4.
  Qn5: Inverted register output 5.
  Qn6: Inverted register output 6.
  Qn7: Inverted register output 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Octal Register
The inverting output makes the 74x564 useful when the downstream logic requires active LOW signals, saving an inverter stage on the bus. It is otherwise pin compatible with the 74x574.
74x566:
The 74x566 is an 8 bit bidirectional registered transceiver with inverting outputs. It is the inverting output version of the 74x546. LEAB and LEBA are latch enables for the two directions; CLK registers the latch output; DIR and OEABn/OEBAn control direction and bus drives. Data is inverted through the register path.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  CLK: Clock for register capture.
  DIR: Direction select.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Inverting vs Non Inverting Registered Transceiver
74x546 passes data unchanged; 74x566 inverts it. Use 74x566 when the receiving bus uses complemented logic or to avoid external inverter stages.
74x567:
The 74x567 is an 8 bit bidirectional latched transceiver with inverting outputs. It is the inverting version of the 74x547. LEAB/LEBA are level triggered (transparent when HIGH); there is no separate CLK. All data paths invert through the latch.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  DIR: Direction select.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  NC: No connect (pin 23).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Latched vs Registered Inverting Transceiver
74x567 (latch) is transparent when LEAB/LEBA are HIGH; 74x566 (registered) requires a CLK edge. Use 74x567 when asynchronous transparent operation is needed.
74x568:
The 74x568 is a synchronous 4 bit BCD (decade) up/down counter with tri state outputs. U_Dn HIGH = count up, LOW = count down. ENP and ENT both HIGH enable counting; LOAD loads the parallel A-D inputs; CLRn (active LOW) synchronously clears on the next CLK edge. RCO carries for multi digit cascading.

Pins:
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW). Clears counter on next CLK edge.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  U_Dn: Up/Down select. HIGH = count up; LOW = count down.
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel. Both ENP and ENT must be HIGH to count.
  A: Parallel load input A (LSB).
  B: Parallel load input B.
  C: Parallel load input C.
  D: Parallel load input D (MSB).
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle. Also gates RCO.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  LOAD: Parallel Load (active LOW). Loads A-D on next CLK.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output for cascade.
  QD: Counter output D.
  QC: Counter output C.
  QB: Counter output B.
  QA: Counter output A.
  NC: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Up/Down BCD Counter
The up/down capability enables presettable timer and event counting circuits. For a down counter, preload the starting value via LOAD; the counter decrements to 0 and then rolls over to 9.
74x569:
The 74x569 is the binary version of the 74x568, counting 0-15 (modulo-16) rather than 0-9. Same synchronous up/down control, ENP/ENT enables, parallel load, synchronous clear (CLRn active LOW), and tri state outputs. RCO cascades for wider counters.

Pins:
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW).
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  U_Dn: Up/Down select.
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel.
  A: Parallel load input A.
  B: Parallel load input B.
  C: Parallel load input C.
  D: Parallel load input D.
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  LOAD: Parallel Load (active LOW).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output.
  QD: Counter output D (MSB).
  QC: Counter output C.
  QB: Counter output B.
  QA: Counter output A (LSB).
  NC: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x568 vs 74x569
74x568 is decade (BCD, 0-9); 74x569 is binary (0-15). Choose 74x568 for decimal display applications; 74x569 for general purpose power of-2 counting.
74x575:
The 74x575 is an octal D type edge triggered flip flop with synchronous clear and tri state outputs. CLRn (active LOW) on the rising CLK edge resets all outputs to 0. OE (active LOW) enables the Q outputs. The 24-pin package provides 3 NC pins for mechanical compatibility.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW). Clears to 0 on rising CLK edge.
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q0: Register output 0.
  Q1: Register output 1.
  Q2: Register output 2.
  Q3: Register output 3.
  Q4: Register output 4.
  Q5: Register output 5.
  Q6: Register output 6.
  Q7: Register output 7.
  NC: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Synchronous Clear
Synchronous clear (vs asynchronous) ensures the register only resets on the active clock edge. This avoids glitches from metastable clear signals in synchronous systems.
74x576:
The 74x576 is an octal D type edge triggered flip flop with inverting outputs and tri state, identical in function to the 74x564. Both capture the complement of D inputs on the rising CLK edge. The part number difference reflects a process or package revision within the ALS/FAST family.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  Qn0: Inverted register output 0.
  Qn1: Inverted register output 1.
  Qn2: Inverted register output 2.
  Qn3: Inverted register output 3.
  Qn4: Inverted register output 4.
  Qn5: Inverted register output 5.
  Qn6: Inverted register output 6.
  Qn7: Inverted register output 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x564 vs 74x576
Both chips are octal inverting registers. The 74x576 is the ALS family update of the 74x564. They are functionally interchangeable in most designs.
74x577:
The 74x577 adds synchronous clear to the inverting output octal D-FF. CLRn LOW on a rising CLK edge forces all Qn HIGH (since outputs are inverted). OEn tri states all outputs. The 24-pin package is pin compatible with 74x575 but with inverted outputs.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLRn: Synchronous Clear (active LOW). Forces Qn HIGH on rising CLK.
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Qn0: Inverted register output 0.
  Qn1: Inverted register output 1.
  Qn2: Inverted register output 2.
  Qn3: Inverted register output 3.
  Qn4: Inverted register output 4.
  Qn5: Inverted register output 5.
  Qn6: Inverted register output 6.
  Qn7: Inverted register output 7.
  NC: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Synchronous Clear with Inverted Outputs
Because outputs are inverted, CLRn LOW clears D=0 into the flip flop, which appears as Qn=HIGH after the CLK edge. For active LOW downstream logic, this acts as a forced HIGH reset.
74x579:
The 74x579 is an 8 bit synchronous binary up/down counter with a bidirectional tri state bus (A0-A7 are shared data/output pins). ENP, ENT, U_Dn, LOAD, and OEn provide full count control. TC (terminal count) goes HIGH at the maximum (count up) or minimum (count down) to cascade multiple chips.

Pins:
  CLK: Clock (rising edge).
  ENT: Count Enable Trickle (also gates TC).
  ENP: Count Enable Parallel.
  U_Dn: Up/Down select. HIGH = up; LOW = down.
  LOAD: Parallel Load (active LOW).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Bidirectional data/output bit 0.
  A1: Bidirectional data/output bit 1.
  A2: Bidirectional data/output bit 2.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  A3: Bidirectional data/output bit 3.
  A4: Bidirectional data/output bit 4.
  A5: Bidirectional data/output bit 5.
  A6: Bidirectional data/output bit 6.
  A7: Bidirectional data/output bit 7.
  TC: Terminal Count output for cascade.
  NC: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

8 bit Up/Down Counter
At 8 bits, the 74x579 counts 0-255. Cascade using TC → ENT of the next chip for a 16 bit or wider counter. The shared A bus allows parallel load directly from the bus.
74x580:
The 74x580 is an octal D type transparent latch with inverting outputs and tri state. LE HIGH makes outputs follow the inverted D inputs; LE LOW holds the latched value. OEn enables outputs. Functionally equivalent to the 74x533/74x563 family.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  D0: Data input 0.
  D1: Data input 1.
  D2: Data input 2.
  D3: Data input 3.
  D4: Data input 4.
  D5: Data input 5.
  D6: Data input 6.
  D7: Data input 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  LE: Latch Enable. HIGH = transparent; LOW = hold.
  Qn0: Inverted latch output 0.
  Qn1: Inverted latch output 1.
  Qn2: Inverted latch output 2.
  Qn3: Inverted latch output 3.
  Qn4: Inverted latch output 4.
  Qn5: Inverted latch output 5.
  Qn6: Inverted latch output 6.
  Qn7: Inverted latch output 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Octal Inverting Transparent Latch
Interchangeable with 74x533 and 74x563. The inverting output saves an external inversion stage when driving active LOW bus signals.
74x582:
The 74x582 is a 4 bit BCD (binary coded decimal) arithmetic logic unit. It performs 16 operations on BCD operands selected by S3-S0, with mode M (arithmetic vs logic). Cn is carry in; F0-F3 are the result; Cn4 is carry out; OVR indicates decimal overflow; P and G are carry propagate and generate outputs for fast lookahead chaining.

Pins:
  B0: BCD operand B bit 0.
  A0: BCD operand A bit 0.
  B1: BCD operand B bit 1.
  A1: BCD operand A bit 1.
  B2: BCD operand B bit 2.
  A2: BCD operand A bit 2.
  B3: BCD operand B bit 3.
  A3: BCD operand A bit 3.
  Cn: Carry input.
  M: Mode select. HIGH = logic; LOW = arithmetic.
  S0: Function select bit 0.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  S1: Function select bit 1.
  S2: Function select bit 2.
  S3: Function select bit 3.
  F0: ALU result bit 0.
  F1: ALU result bit 1.
  F2: ALU result bit 2.
  F3: ALU result bit 3.
  Cn4: Carry output.
  OVR: BCD overflow output.
  P: Carry propagate output for lookahead.
  G: Carry generate output for lookahead.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

BCD ALU vs Binary ALU
The 74x582 performs the same 16 operations as the 74x181 but corrects the BCD carry internally. This avoids the BCD correction step required when doing decimal arithmetic with binary ALUs.
Note: Simplification: only the BCD-add operation is modelled in 74Sim (mode M=LOW with S3..S0 = 1001), which produces F = A + B + Cn with decimal carry on Cn4. Every other select/mode combination simply passes A through to F, and the OVR, P and G outputs are always held LOW. Treat this part as a decimal adder in-sim, not a full 16-function ALU.
74x583:
The 74x583 is a 4 bit BCD adder that sums two BCD digits and a carry in to produce a 4 bit BCD sum and a carry out. It is simpler than the 74x582 (addition only, no function select). Cascade by connecting Cout to Cin of the next chip for multi digit decimal addition.

Pins:
  B0: BCD addend B bit 0.
  A0: BCD addend A bit 0.
  B1: BCD addend B bit 1.
  A1: BCD addend A bit 1.
  B2: BCD addend B bit 2.
  A2: BCD addend A bit 2.
  B3: BCD addend B bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  A3: BCD addend A bit 3.
  Cin: Carry input.
  Cout: Carry output.
  S3: BCD sum bit 3.
  S2: BCD sum bit 2.
  S1: BCD sum bit 1.
  S0: BCD sum bit 0.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

BCD Addition
Adds two BCD digits (0-9 each) and produce a BCD result plus carry. The chip automatically handles the 6-correction needed to keep the result valid BCD, unlike a plain binary adder.
74x588:
The 74x588 is an 8 bit bidirectional bus transceiver designed for IEEE-488 (GPIB) bus interfaces. It integrates bus termination resistors matched to GPIB cable impedance requirements. DIR and OEn control direction and output enable, identical to a 74x245, but internal termination simplifies GPIB board design.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

IEEE-488 / GPIB Bus
IEEE-488 (also called GPIB or HP IB) is an 8 bit parallel instrumentation bus. The bus uses passive termination resistors to defined impedance. The 74x588 combines the driver/receiver function of a 74x245 with the required termination, reducing external component count.
74x589:
The 74x589 is an 8 bit serial in/parallel out shift register with a parallel input latch. D0-D7 are first captured into a transparent latch when RCK is HIGH. On each rising SRCK edge (gated by CKEN), data is shifted into the shift register from SER. QH is the serial output (last stage). OE (active LOW) enables QH. Useful for converting a parallel bus byte into a serial stream.

Pins:
  QH: Serial output (MSB of shift register). Enabled by OE.
  SER: Serial data input. Enters the first stage on each SRCK edge.
  OE: Output Enable for QH (active LOW).
  D7: Parallel input bit 7 (MSB) to input latch.
  D6: Parallel input bit 6.
  D5: Parallel input bit 5.
  D4: Parallel input bit 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  D3: Parallel input bit 3.
  D2: Parallel input bit 2.
  D1: Parallel input bit 1.
  D0: Parallel input bit 0.
  SRCK: Shift register clock (rising edge).
  RCK: Register Clock. HIGH = transparent, captures D0-D7 into latch.
  CKEN: Clock Enable for SRCK (active LOW). Tie LOW to enable shifting.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Parallel In Serial Out via Input Latch
The input latch decouples the D0-D7 bus from the shift register. First, assert RCK HIGH to capture the parallel byte; then shift out 8 bits serially via SRCK. The latch holds the value stable during shifting even if the D bus changes.
74x590:
The 74x590 is an 8 bit binary counter (0-255) with a separate 8 bit output register and tri state outputs. CCLKn (active LOW falling edge) increments the counter; RCLK rising edge latches the counter value into the output register. OEn (active LOW) enables the register outputs. Separate CCLR and RCLR clear the counter and register independently.

Pins:
  Q1: Output register bit 1.
  Q2: Output register bit 2.
  Q3: Output register bit 3.
  Q4: Output register bit 4.
  Q5: Output register bit 5.
  Q6: Output register bit 6.
  Q7: Output register bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RC: Ripple Carry out (HIGH when counter = 255).
  CCLKn: Counter Clock (active LOW, falling edge).
  CCLR: Async Counter Clear (active HIGH). Clears counter immediately.
  RCLK: Register Clock (rising edge). Latches counter to output register.
  RCLR: Async Register Clear (active LOW).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  Q0: Output register bit 0 (LSB).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Counter with Output Register
The separate output register lets you freeze a snapshot of the count while the counter continues running. Pulse RCLK to capture the current count; the bus shows that value until the next RCLK.
74x591:
The 74x591 is functionally identical to the 74x590 but with open collector outputs instead of tri state. The OEn pin still exists; with OC outputs, external pull up resistors are required. This allows wire OR combinations on shared bus lines.

Pins:
  Q1: Open collector output bit 1.
  Q2: Open collector output bit 2.
  Q3: Open collector output bit 3.
  Q4: Open collector output bit 4.
  Q5: Open collector output bit 5.
  Q6: Open collector output bit 6.
  Q7: Open collector output bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RC: Ripple Carry out.
  CCLKn: Counter Clock (active LOW).
  CCLR: Async Counter Clear.
  RCLK: Register Clock (rising edge).
  RCLR: Async Register Clear.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  Q0: Open collector output bit 0.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

74x590 vs 74x591
74x590 has tri state outputs; 74x591 has open collector. Use 74x591 with pull ups when multiple counters share a bus via wire OR, or when driving optocouplers or relay drivers directly.
Note: External pull up resistors (typically 1-4.7 kΩ) are required on all Q outputs for proper HIGH levels.
74x592:
The 74x592 is an 8 bit binary counter with an input register for parallel loading. D0-D7 are captured by the input register on rising RCK. When CKEN=0, the next rising CCK loads the input register value into the counter; when CKEN=1, CCK increments the counter. RC indicates counter = 255. No parallel output pins (counter drives internal logic only).

Pins:
  D1: Parallel input bit 1.
  D2: Parallel input bit 2.
  D3: Parallel input bit 3.
  D4: Parallel input bit 4.
  D5: Parallel input bit 5.
  D6: Parallel input bit 6.
  D7: Parallel input bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RC: Ripple Carry (counter = 255).
  CCK: Counter Clock (rising edge).
  CCLR: Async Counter Clear (active LOW).
  RCK: Register Clock. Rising edge captures D0-D7.
  CKEN: Count Enable. HIGH = count; LOW = load on next CCK.
  D0: Parallel input bit 0.
  VCC: Positive supply (pin 15).
  GND2: Additional ground pin (pin 16).

Counter with Input Register
The input register separates the data bus from the counter. Load a preset value: first capture it with RCK, then load it with CKEN=0 on the next CCK. After loading, set CKEN=1 to resume counting from the loaded value.
74x593:
The 74x593 is the tri state output version of the 74x592. D0-D7 are bidirectional: used as inputs for parallel load (via RCK) and as outputs (Q0-D7) when OEn is LOW. Q4-Q7 are dedicated output pins. RC is the ripple carry. CKEN selects load vs count on each CCK edge.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  D0: Bidirectional data/output bit 0.
  D1: Bidirectional data/output bit 1.
  D2: Bidirectional data/output bit 2.
  D3: Bidirectional data/output bit 3.
  D4: Bidirectional data/output bit 4.
  D5: Bidirectional data/output bit 5.
  D6: Bidirectional data/output bit 6.
  D7: Bidirectional data/output bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  RCK: Register Clock. Rising edge captures D0-D7.
  CCK: Counter Clock (rising edge).
  CCLR: Async Counter Clear (active LOW).
  CKEN: Count Enable. HIGH = count; LOW = load on next CCK.
  RC: Ripple Carry output.
  Q7: Counter output bit 7.
  Q6: Counter output bit 6.
  Q5: Counter output bit 5.
  Q4: Counter output bit 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x592 vs 74x593
74x592 has no output pins; 74x593 adds tri state outputs on D0-D7 and dedicated Q4-Q7 outputs. Use 74x593 when counter contents must be read back over a shared bus.
74x594:
The 74x594 is an 8 bit serial in/parallel out shift register with a separate 8 bit output latch. SER data is shifted on rising SRCLK edges; the current shift register state is latched to outputs QA-QH on rising RCLK. Unlike the 74x595, there is no OEn tri state outputs are always actively driven. SRCLR clears the shift register; RCLR clears the output latch.

Pins:
  QB: Output latch bit B.
  QC: Output latch bit C.
  QD: Output latch bit D.
  QE: Output latch bit E.
  QF: Output latch bit F.
  QG: Output latch bit G.
  QH: Output latch bit H (MSB of shift register).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  QHs: Serial output (cascade to next chip SER input).
  SRCLR: Shift Register Clear (active LOW, asynchronous).
  SRCLK: Shift Register Clock (rising edge).
  RCLK: Register Clock (rising edge). Transfers shift reg to output latch.
  RCLR: Output Register Clear (active LOW, asynchronous).
  SER: Serial data input.
  QA: Output latch bit A (LSB).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Two Stage Shift Register
The two stage design (shift register → output latch) prevents glitches on the outputs during shifting. All outputs update simultaneously on RCLK, not one bit at a time as the shift register loads.
74x596:
The 74x596 is an 8 bit SIPO shift register with a two stage output latch and open collector outputs. Same SRCLK/RCLK/SRCLR shift then latch operation as the 74x595, but outputs are open collector requiring external pull ups. OEn (active LOW) enables the outputs.

Pins:
  QB: OC output bit B.
  QC: OC output bit C.
  QD: OC output bit D.
  QE: OC output bit E.
  QF: OC output bit F.
  QG: OC output bit G.
  QH: OC output bit H.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  QHs: Serial cascade output.
  SRCLR: Shift Register Clear (active LOW).
  SRCLK: Shift Register Clock.
  RCLK: Output Register Clock.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  SER: Serial input.
  QA: OC output bit A.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Open Collector SIPO Register
OC outputs allow wire OR of multiple shift registers on a common bus and direct driving of high voltage or high current loads. External pull ups (1-4.7 kΩ) are required.
74x597:
The 74x597 reads 8 bits in parallel and streams them out one at a time on a single serial pin the input-side mirror of the 74x595, which does the reverse. It has two stages inside: a storage latch that grabs pins A H on a rising edge of RCK, and a shift register the latched byte is loaded into and clocked out of. Pull SRLOAD low to copy the latch into the shift register, then clock SRCK to shift the byte out on QHs, the H bit first. SER feeds bits in from another chip, so several 597s chain into a wider input. It is the classic way to read a bank of switches or sensors into a microcontroller over just a few wires. Two things trip people up: there are two separate clocks (RCK loads the latch, SRCK shifts), and SRLOAD and SRCLR are both active low and asynchronous they act the instant they go low, no clock needed.

Pins:
  B: Parallel data input B (pin 1).
  C: Parallel data input C (pin 2).
  D: Parallel data input D (pin 3).
  E: Parallel data input E (pin 4).
  F: Parallel data input F (pin 5).
  G: Parallel data input G (pin 6).
  H: Parallel data input H the last shift stage, read out first on QHs (pin 7).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  QHs: Serial output the H (last) stage of the shift register. The datasheet labels it QH′; wire it to SER of the next chip to cascade (pin 9).
  SRCLR: Shift Register Clear. A LOW clears the shift register to all zeros at once, no clock needed (asynchronous, active LOW, pin 10).
  SRCK: Shift Register Clock. A rising edge shifts the register one place, but only while SRLOAD and SRCLR are HIGH (pin 11).
  RCK: Register Clock. A rising edge captures inputs A H into the storage latch (pin 12).
  SRLOAD: Shift Register Load. A LOW copies the storage latch into the shift register at once, no clock needed (asynchronous, active LOW, pin 13).
  SER: Serial data input. New bits enter here at stage A during a shift; used to cascade chips (pin 14).
  A: Parallel data input A the first shift stage, next to SER (pin 15).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Two stages: latch, then shift register
The 597 is really two 8 bit registers back to back. The first is a storage latch: on each rising edge of RCK it takes a snapshot of the eight parallel inputs A H and holds it. Nothing shifts yet this just freezes the inputs so they can not change under you while you read them out.
