The second is the shift register, the part that produces the serial output. Pulling SRLOAD low copies the latched snapshot into it in one step no clock edge, the load is asynchronous. After that, every rising edge of SRCK moves the whole register one place toward QHs. The bit that falls off the end appears on QHs; the bit that enters the other end comes from SER.
Splitting the capture (RCK) from the shift-out (SRCK) lets you grab a fresh snapshot at a precise moment and then read the previous one out at your own pace on a separate clock.

Control inputs
RCK and SRCK are edge triggered (they act on a rising edge). SRLOAD and SRCLR are level sensitive and asynchronous: they take effect the moment they go low and stay in effect the whole time they are held low. SRCLR wins over SRLOAD hold both low and you get zeros, not the latched byte.
  SRCLR=0                      → shift register cleared to 0 (async, overrides all)
  SRCLR=1, SRLOAD=0            → load storage latch into shift register (async)
  SRCLR=1, SRLOAD=1, SRCK↑     → shift one place toward QHs (SER enters at A)
  RCK↑                         → latch A H into storage (works at any time)

Reading a byte out
  - Put the 8 bits on A H and pulse RCK high to latch them.
  - Pulse SRLOAD low (then back high) to copy the latched byte into the shift register.
  - QHs now shows bit H. Each SRCK rising edge then brings the next bit to QHs, in the order H, G, F, … A.
  - RCK can re-latch a fresh A H at any time without disturbing a shift already in progress.

Common uses
  - Reading a bank of 8 switches, jumpers, or sensor outputs into a microcontroller over ~4 wires instead of 8.
  - Chaining several 597s (QHs → SER of the next) to read 16, 24, or more inputs on the same few wires.
  - Snapshotting a fast parallel bus at one instant with RCK, then clocking it into a slower processor at leisure with SRCK.
  - Pairing with a 74x595 (serial out) to build a serial input/output port from two shift registers.

Gotchas
  - Two clocks, two jobs: RCK loads the input latch, SRCK shifts. Pulsing the wrong one does nothing useful.
  - SRLOAD and SRCLR are active LOW and asynchronous no clock edge, and they hold as long as the pin is low. Tie them high when not in use.
  - SRCLR beats SRLOAD: hold both low and the register clears to zero instead of loading the latch.
  - H comes out first, A last. If your bytes read back bit-reversed, that ordering is why.
  - The datasheet names the parallel inputs A H and the serial output QH′; this simulator shows QH′ as QHs.
74x598:
The 74x598 is an 8 bit shift register with selectable modes. S1/S0 control the operation: hold, shift right, parallel load from the input latch (captured on RCK), or parallel output onto the D bus. OEn enables the D bus output. Provides the most flexible shift register operation of the 74x59x family.

Pins:
  SER: Serial input for shift right mode.
  D0: Bidirectional data bus bit 0.
  D1: Bidirectional data bus bit 1.
  D2: Bidirectional data bus bit 2.
  D3: Bidirectional data bus bit 3.
  D4: Bidirectional data bus bit 4.
  D5: Bidirectional data bus bit 5.
  D6: Bidirectional data bus bit 6.
  D7: Bidirectional data bus bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  OEn: Output Enable for D0-D7 bus (active LOW).
  CLK: Shift register clock (rising edge).
  S0: Mode select bit 0.
  S1: Mode select bit 1.
  RCK: Register Clock for input latch.
  QH: Serial output bit H.
  QG: Serial output bit G.
  QF: Serial output bit F.
  QE: Serial output bit E.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Mode Select
S1=0, S0=0: Hold. S1=0, S0=1: Shift right. S1=1, S0=0: Parallel load from latch. S1=1, S0=1: Parallel output to D bus.
74x599:
The 74x599 is an 8 bit SIPO shift register with two stage output latch and open collector outputs. Similar to the 74x596 but replaces OEn with RCLR (output register clear, active LOW). Outputs are always enabled (OC style). SRCLR clears the shift register.

Pins:
  QB: OC output bit B.
  QC: OC output bit C.
  QD: OC output bit D.
  QE: OC output bit E.
  QF: OC output bit F.
  QG: OC output bit G.
  QH: OC output bit H.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  QHs: Serial cascade output.
  SRCLR: Shift Register Clear (active LOW).
  SRCLK: Shift Register Clock.
  RCLK: Output Register Clock.
  RCLR: Output Register Clear (active LOW). Clears output latch.
  SER: Serial input.
  QA: OC output bit A.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

74x596 vs 74x599
Both are OC SIPO registers. 74x596 has OEn (disable all outputs); 74x599 has RCLR (clear output latch asynchronously). Choose 74x599 when you need hardware reset of the output latch rather than high impedance.
74x600:
The 74x600 is a DRAM refresh controller for 4K and 16K DRAM devices. It generates periodic RAS (Row Address Strobe) and CAS (Column Address Strobe) refresh cycles based on an input oscillator (OSC). BURST mode performs a run of refreshes back to back; transparent mode steals a single cycle. TC indicates terminal count of the refresh address counter.

Pins:
  OSC: Oscillator input. Controls refresh timing.
  IN0: Address input bit 0 for multiplexed address output.
  IN1: Address input bit 1.
  IN2: Address input bit 2.
  IN3: Address input bit 3.
  IN4: Address input bit 4.
  IN5: Address input bit 5.
  IN6: Address input bit 6.
  TC: Terminal Count output. HIGH when refresh counter wraps.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  RAS: Row Address Strobe output for DRAM.
  CAS: Column Address Strobe output for DRAM.
  RFSH: Refresh indicator output.
  BURST: Burst mode select. HIGH = run all refresh cycles consecutively.
  MR: Master Reset (active LOW).
  OUT4: Multiplexed address output bit 4.
  OUT5: Multiplexed address output bit 5.
  OUT6: Multiplexed address output bit 6.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

DRAM Refresh
DRAM cells lose their charge if not periodically refreshed. The 74x600 automates this: it cycles through all row addresses, issuing RAS strobes. Burst mode does all refreshes in one block; transparent mode interleaves refreshes with normal memory access.
74x601:
The 74x601 is a DRAM refresh controller for 64K DRAM devices. It requires a 7 bit row address counter (vs the 6 bit used for 4K/16K in the 74x600). All other features (BURST mode, transparent mode, RAS/CAS generation) are identical to the 74x600.

Pins:
  OSC: Oscillator input.
  IN0: Address input bit 0.
  IN1: Address input bit 1.
  IN2: Address input bit 2.
  IN3: Address input bit 3.
  IN4: Address input bit 4.
  IN5: Address input bit 5.
  IN6: Address input bit 6.
  TC: Terminal Count.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  RAS: Row Address Strobe.
  CAS: Column Address Strobe.
  RFSH: Refresh indicator.
  BURST: Burst mode select.
  MR: Master Reset.
  OUT4: Address output bit 4.
  OUT5: Address output bit 5.
  OUT6: Address output bit 6.
  OEn: Output Enable.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x600 vs 74x601
74x600 handles 4K/16K DRAM (64 or 128 rows); 74x601 handles 64K DRAM (256 rows), requiring a 7 bit refresh counter. Choose based on the DRAM capacity in the system.
74x602:
The 74x602 is a DRAM refresh controller for 4K/16K DRAM using cycle steal mode. Unlike transparent mode, cycle steal actively halts the CPU bus to perform a refresh cycle. BURST mode runs consecutive refreshes. Pin compatible with 74x600 but with different internal refresh arbitration logic.

Pins:
  OSC: Oscillator input.
  IN0: Address input bit 0.
  IN1: Address input bit 1.
  IN2: Address input bit 2.
  IN3: Address input bit 3.
  IN4: Address input bit 4.
  IN5: Address input bit 5.
  IN6: Address input bit 6.
  TC: Terminal Count.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  RAS: Row Address Strobe.
  CAS: Column Address Strobe.
  RFSH: Refresh indicator.
  BURST: Burst mode select.
  MR: Master Reset.
  OUT4: Address output bit 4.
  OUT5: Address output bit 5.
  OUT6: Address output bit 6.
  OEn: Output Enable.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Cycle Steal vs Transparent Refresh
Cycle steal refresh asserts a HOLD/WAIT signal to the CPU, pausing the bus for one cycle to perform a refresh. Transparent mode attempts to sneak a refresh into unused bus cycles without stalling the CPU.
74x603:
The 74x603 is the 64K DRAM version of the cycle steal refresh controller (74x602). It handles 256 row refresh cycles for 64K DRAM. Cycle steal mode halts the CPU for each refresh; BURST performs all refreshes consecutively.

Pins:
  OSC: Oscillator input.
  IN0: Address input bit 0.
  IN1: Address input bit 1.
  IN2: Address input bit 2.
  IN3: Address input bit 3.
  IN4: Address input bit 4.
  IN5: Address input bit 5.
  IN6: Address input bit 6.
  TC: Terminal Count.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  RAS: Row Address Strobe.
  CAS: Column Address Strobe.
  RFSH: Refresh indicator.
  BURST: Burst mode.
  MR: Master Reset.
  OUT4: Address output bit 4.
  OUT5: Address output bit 5.
  OUT6: Address output bit 6.
  OEn: Output Enable.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

DRAM Refresh Family
  - 74x600: 4K/16K, transparent mode
  - 74x601: 64K, transparent mode
  - 74x602: 4K/16K, cycle steal mode
  - 74x603: 64K, cycle steal mode
74x608:
The 74x608 is a memory cycle controller that generates control signals (CS, OE, WE, WAIT) from CPU address and RD/WR signals. EN1 and EN2 enable the decode; CLK synchronizes cycle generation; MR resets the state machine. Useful for glue logic between a CPU and SRAM or EPROM in a breadboard microcomputer.

Pins:
  A0: Address input bit 0.
  A1: Address input bit 1.
  A2: Address input bit 2.
  A3: Address input bit 3.
  EN1: Enable 1 (active HIGH).
  EN2: Enable 2 (active LOW).
  RD: Read strobe from CPU.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  WR: Write strobe from CPU.
  CS: Chip Select output for memory device.
  OE: Output Enable output for memory device.
  WE: Write Enable output for memory device.
  CLK: Clock for cycle synchronization.
  MR: Master Reset.
  WAIT: Wait output to CPU (extends bus cycle).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Memory Bus Glue Logic
The 74x608 reduces the discrete logic needed to interface a CPU to memory by integrating address decode, read/write qualification, and wait state generation into one chip.
Note: Simplification: 74Sim models only the combinational enable path CS goes active (LOW) when EN1=EN2=LOW and MR=HIGH; OE follows CS AND RD; WE follows CS AND WR. The address inputs A0..A3, the CLK-synchronised cycle state machine, and wait state generation are not modelled WAIT is always held inactive (HIGH).
74x614:
The 74x614 is an octal registered bidirectional transceiver with inverting open collector outputs. It has the same OEABn/OEBAn/LEAB/LEBA/CLK/DIR control structure as the 74x566 (tri state), but outputs are open collector. External pull up resistors are required. Useful for wired AND bus designs.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  CLK: Register clock.
  DIR: Direction select.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x566 vs 74x614
74x566 has tri state outputs; 74x614 has open collector. Both invert data and use the same LEAB/LEBA/CLK/DIR control scheme. Use 74x614 for wired AND multi master bus architectures.
Note: External pull up resistors required on all bidirectional A/B pins.
74x615:
The 74x615 is an octal registered bidirectional transceiver with non inverting open collector outputs. It is the OC version of the 74x543. LEAB/LEBA are latch enables, CLK registers the output, and DIR/OEABn/OEBAn control direction and drive. Open collector outputs require external pull ups.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A.
  CLK: Register clock.
  DIR: Direction select.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x543 vs 74x615
74x543 has tri state outputs; 74x615 has open collector. Both are non inverting registered transceivers with the same control structure.
Note: External pull up resistors required on all A/B port pins.
74x620:
The 74x620 is an 8 bit bidirectional inverting bus transceiver with tri state outputs. DIR HIGH routes A to inverted B; DIR LOW routes B to inverted A. OE (active LOW) enables the drive. It is the inverting output complement of the 74x245.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives inverted A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting Bidirectional Transceiver
Use the 74x620 when the receiving bus uses complemented active LOW logic, saving an inverter layer after the transceiver.
74x621:
The 74x621 is an 8 bit bidirectional non inverting bus transceiver with open collector outputs. Same DIR/OE (active LOW) interface as the 74x245 but with OC outputs requiring external pull ups. Useful for wire OR bus sharing or driving optocouplers.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

OC Bidirectional Transceiver
Open collector enables multiple 74x621s to share the same bus lines via wired AND. External pull up resistors (1-4.7 kΩ) are required.
74x622:
The 74x622 is an 8 bit bidirectional inverting bus transceiver with open collector outputs. It is the OC version of the 74x620. All data is inverted through the transceiver; external pull ups required.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives inverted A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x620/621/622/623 Family
  - 74x620: TRI outputs, inverting
  - 74x621: OC outputs, non inverting
  - 74x622: OC outputs, inverting
  - 74x623: TRI outputs, non inverting
74x623:
The 74x623 is an 8 bit bidirectional non inverting bus transceiver with tri state outputs, functionally equivalent to the 74x245. DIR controls direction; OE (active LOW) enables the outputs. A pin for pin compatible replacement for 74x245 in many designs.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Non Inverting Transceiver
Functionally the same as the 74x245 the standard bidirectional bus transceiver. Select 74x623 vs 74x245 based on logic family speed or availability.
74x624:
The 74x624 is a voltage controlled oscillator (VCO). An analog voltage on FREQ1/FREQ2 controls the output frequency; RNG selects a coarse frequency range; OE (active LOW) enables the two complementary outputs (OUT and OUTn). In the simulator the oscillator runs behaviorally: FREQ1 voltage sets the frequency (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz, RNG ×10), and pulling OEn LOW enables the outputs.

Pins:
  FREQ1: Frequency control input 1 (analog voltage).
  FREQ2: Frequency control input 2 (analog voltage).
  RNG: Range select. Selects one of several frequency bands.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  OUT: Oscillator output.
  OUTn: Complementary oscillator output.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  FSEL: Frequency select.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  AIN3: Analog input 3.
  AIN4: Analog input 4.
  SYNC: Sync input for phase locking.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

Voltage Controlled Oscillator
A VCO produces a digital clock whose frequency is proportional to an analog control voltage. Typical applications include phase locked loops (PLLs), frequency synthesizers, and clock generators.
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x625:
The 74x625 contains two independent voltage controlled oscillators in a single 16-pin package. Each VCO has separate FREQ1/FREQ2 and RNG control inputs and complementary outputs. Useful when two independent clock sources are needed on the same board.

Pins:
  FREQ1A: Frequency control 1 for VCO A.
  FREQ2A: Frequency control 2 for VCO A.
  RNGA: Range select for VCO A.
  OUTA: Output of VCO A.
  OUTnA: Complementary output of VCO A.
  OUTB: Output of VCO B.
  OUTnB: Complementary output of VCO B.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  FREQ1B: Frequency control 1 for VCO B.
  FREQ2B: Frequency control 2 for VCO B.
  RNGB: Range select for VCO B.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  AIN3: Analog input 3.
  AIN4: Analog input 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Dual VCO
The 74x625 integrates two VCOs, reducing board space compared to two 74x624 chips. Both VCOs are analog and not simulatable.
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x626:
The 74x626 is a dual VCO with per section enable pins (OEnA, OEnB). Each VCO has independent FREQ1/FREQ2 controls. The enable pins allow the outputs to be disabled without removing power, useful in frequency switching applications.

Pins:
  FREQ1A: Frequency control 1 for VCO A.
  FREQ2A: Frequency control 2 for VCO A.
  OEnA: Output Enable for VCO A (active LOW).
  OUTA: Output of VCO A.
  OUTnA: Complementary output of VCO A.
  OUTB: Output of VCO B.
  OUTnB: Complementary output of VCO B.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  OEnB: Output Enable for VCO B (active LOW).
  FREQ1B: Frequency control 1 for VCO B.
  FREQ2B: Frequency control 2 for VCO B.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  AIN3: Analog input 3.
  AIN4: Analog input 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Dual VCO with Enable
Adding OEn to each VCO section allows dynamic selection of which oscillator drives the output bus, useful in PLLs that switch between reference and feedback paths.
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x627:
The 74x627 is a dual VCO in a 14-pin package (vs the 74x625 in 16-pin). It has two independent VCO sections with FREQ1/FREQ2 frequency control inputs and complementary outputs. No enable pin is provided. Analog device not simulatable.

Pins:
  FREQ1A: Frequency control 1 for VCO A.
  FREQ2A: Frequency control 2 for VCO A.
  OUTA: Output of VCO A.
  OUTnA: Complementary output of VCO A.
  OUTB: Output of VCO B.
  OUTnB: Complementary output of VCO B.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  FREQ1B: Frequency control 1 for VCO B.
  FREQ2B: Frequency control 2 for VCO B.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  AIN3: Analog input 3.
  AIN4: Analog input 4.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

74x625 vs 74x627
74x625 is 16-pin; 74x627 is 14-pin. The 74x625 adds a RNG (range) pin per section. Choose 74x627 when RNG is not needed and package size is critical.
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x628:
The 74x628 is a single VCO with external temperature compensation (TC pin). It adds a TC pin for connecting an external NTC/PTC thermistor network to compensate for frequency drift with temperature. Otherwise functionally similar to the 74x624 with OEn enable and complementary outputs.

Pins:
  FREQ1: Frequency control input 1.
  FREQ2: Frequency control input 2.
  RNG: Range select.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  OUT: Oscillator output.
  OUTn: Complementary output.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 7).
  TC: Temperature compensation input.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  AIN3: Analog input 3.
  AIN4: Analog input 4.
  SYNC: Sync input.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 14).

74x624 vs 74x628
74x628 adds a TC (temperature compensation) pin to the 74x624 for improved frequency stability over temperature using an external component network.
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x629:
The 74x629 is a dual VCO with both enable (OEn) and range (RNG) control per section. It is the most feature complete of the VCO family combining the enable of 74x626 with the range select of 74x625. Analog device, not simulatable.

Pins:
  FREQ1A: Frequency control 1 for VCO A.
  FREQ2A: Frequency control 2 for VCO A.
  RNGA: Range select for VCO A.
  OEnA: Output Enable for VCO A (active LOW).
  OUTA: Output of VCO A.
  OUTnA: Complementary output of VCO A.
  OUTB: Output of VCO B.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  OUTnB: Complementary output of VCO B.
  OEnB: Output Enable for VCO B (active LOW).
  RNGB: Range select for VCO B.
  FREQ1B: Frequency control 1 for VCO B.
  FREQ2B: Frequency control 2 for VCO B.
  AIN1: Analog input 1.
  AIN2: Analog input 2.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

VCO Family Comparison
  - 74x624/628: single VCO, enable + range (628 adds temp comp)
  - 74x625/627: dual VCO, range only (627 is smaller package)
  - 74x626: dual VCO, enable only
  - 74x629: dual VCO, enable + range (most complete)
Note: Simulated behaviorally: the output toggles at a frequency set by the FREQ pin voltage (0 V ≈ 10 Hz, 5 V ≈ 1 kHz; RNG ×10 where present), and an active LOW OEn (where present) halts the oscillator with OUT held LOW.
74x636:
The 74x636 is an 8 bit parallel error detection and correction (SECDED) circuit with tri state outputs. It generates and checks 7 check bits (CB0-CB6) for 8 data bits, detecting and correcting single bit errors. fE indicates an uncorrectable double bit error. OEn and SEn control output enables. This is too complex to simulate digitally stub only.

Pins:
  D0: Data bit 0.
  D1: Data bit 1.
  D2: Data bit 2.
  D3: Data bit 3.
  D4: Data bit 4.
  D5: Data bit 5.
  D6: Data bit 6.
  D7: Data bit 7.
  CB0: Check bit 0.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CB1: Check bit 1.
  CB2: Check bit 2.
  CB3: Check bit 3.
  CB4: Check bit 4.
  CB5: Check bit 5.
  CB6: Check bit 6.
  fE: Fatal Error output. HIGH = uncorrectable error detected.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  SEn: Syndrome Enable (active LOW). Enables syndrome logic.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

SECDED Error Correction
SECDED (Single Error Correct, Double Error Detect) uses Hamming code extended with a parity bit. The 74x636 implements this for 8 bit memory bytes, generating and checking 7 check bits per byte.
Note: Simulator implements SEC-DED behaviorally: write mode generates the 7 check bits, read mode computes the syndrome and asserts fE on uncorrectable (even-weight) syndromes. The internal H matrix is a clean SEC-DED code, not the bit-exact Hsiao matrix used by TI silicon, so injected bit-flip patterns may not reproduce the exact check bits a real chip produces. Active single bit correction (driving the data line) is not modeled.
74x637:
The 74x637 is the open collector version of the 74x636 SECDED ECC chip. Identical function (8 bit data + 7 check bits, single error correct, double error detect) but with OC outputs that require pull up resistors. OC allows wire OR of the error flag lines in multi bank ECC memory designs.

Pins:
  D0: Data bit 0.
  D1: Data bit 1.
  D2: Data bit 2.
  D3: Data bit 3.
  D4: Data bit 4.
  D5: Data bit 5.
  D6: Data bit 6.
  D7: Data bit 7.
  CB0: Check bit 0.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CB1: Check bit 1.
  CB2: Check bit 2.
  CB3: Check bit 3.
  CB4: Check bit 4.
  CB5: Check bit 5.
  CB6: Check bit 6.
  fE: Fatal Error output (OC).
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  SEn: Syndrome Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x636 vs 74x637
74x636 has tri state outputs; 74x637 has open collector. Use 74x637 when the fE flag needs to be wire OR’d across multiple ECC chips in a memory system.
Note: Same SEC-DED model as the 74x636; only the output drive style (open collector) differs.
74x638:
The 74x638 is an 8 bit inverting bidirectional transceiver where the A-side outputs are tri state and the B-side outputs are open collector. This mixed output structure was designed for interfacing between a tri state bus (e.g., CPU bus) and an open collector bus (e.g., legacy TTL bus).

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A (TRI) drives inverted B (OC); LOW = B drives inverted A.
  A1: Port A bit 1 (tri state).
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8 (open collector).
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Mixed TRI/OC Output Transceiver
The A-side tri state interface suits modern buses; the B-side OC interface suits wire OR legacy buses. The inverting output handles active LOW bus conventions.
Note: B-side outputs require external pull up resistors.
74x639:
The 74x639 is an 8 bit non inverting bidirectional transceiver with mixed outputs: A-side is tri state, B-side is open collector. Same DIR/OEn interface as the 74x638 but without output inversion. For non inverting mixed output bus bridging.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A (TRI) drives B (OC); LOW = B drives A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8 (open collector).
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x638 vs 74x639
74x638 inverts data through the transceiver; 74x639 passes data non inverted. Both have TRI A-side and OC B-side.
74x640:
The 74x640 is an 8 bit bidirectional inverting bus transceiver with tri state outputs, functionally equivalent to the 74x620. DIR controls direction and OEn enables the outputs. Data is inverted as it passes through in either direction.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives inverted A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x620 vs 74x640
Both 74x620 and 74x640 are inverting tri state bidirectional transceivers. They are functionally identical; the part number difference reflects the logic family or manufacturer variant.
74x641:
The 74x641 is an 8 bit bidirectional non inverting bus transceiver with open collector outputs. It is functionally equivalent to the 74x245 but with OC outputs, and interchangeable with the 74x621. DIR controls direction; OE (active LOW) enables the bus. External pull ups required on all A/B data pins.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). Pull HIGH to tri state all outputs.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x641 74x645 Family
  - 74x641: OC outputs, non inverting
  - 74x642: OC outputs, inverting
  - 74x643: TRI outputs, true/inverting
  - 74x644: OC outputs, true/inverting
  - 74x645: TRI outputs, non inverting (most common)
Note: OC variants require external pull up resistors.
74x642:
The 74x642 is an 8 bit bidirectional inverting bus transceiver with open collector outputs. Data is inverted as it passes through in either direction. External pull ups required. Complement of the 74x641.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives inverted A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Inverting OC Transceiver
Use 74x642 when the target bus uses complemented active LOW logic, avoiding an external inverter stage after the transceiver. Pull up resistors required on all bus pins.
74x643:
The 74x643 is an 8 bit bus transceiver with tri state outputs and asymmetric (true/inverting) data paths. When DIR=HIGH, A drives B with inverted data; when DIR=LOW, B drives A non inverted. This asymmetry suits bus interfaces where one side requires complemented logic.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives A (non inverted).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

True/Inverting Data Path
Unlike symmetric transceivers (74x245, 74x641), the 74x643 inverts only when DIR=HIGH (A→B). The reverse path (B→A) is non inverting. This saves an inverter when bridging a TTL active HIGH bus to an active LOW backplane.
74x644:
The 74x644 is the open collector version of the 74x643. It has the same true/inverting asymmetric data paths (A→B inverting, B→A non inverting) but with OC outputs requiring pull ups. Wire OR capability on the inverting output path.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B (OC); LOW = B drives A (non inverted).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

74x643 vs 74x644
74x643 has tri state outputs; 74x644 has open collector. Both have the same true/inverting asymmetric data path behavior.
Note: External pull ups required on all B-side port pins.
74x645:
The 74x645 is an 8 bit bidirectional non inverting bus transceiver with tri state outputs. Functionally equivalent to the 74x245, it is the most common member of the 74x641-645 family. DIR controls direction; OE (active LOW) enables outputs.

Pins:
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  A8: Port A bit 8.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  B8: Port B bit 8.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Standard 8 bit Bus Transceiver
74x645 is a pin compatible drop-in for the 74x245. Both are non inverting tri state bidirectional transceivers used ubiquitously to buffer data buses in 8 bit microcomputer and peripheral designs.
74x646:
The 74x646 is a 24-pin registered bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. It contains two pipeline stages: clock controlled latches (CLAB for A→B, CLBA for B→A) and a shared output register (CLK). DIR and OEABn/OEBAn control direction and output enables. This allows pipelined handoff between two data buses.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLAB: Clock Latch A-to B. Rising edge captures A-bus data into the A→B latch.
  CLBA: Clock Latch B-to A. Rising edge captures B-bus data into the B→A latch.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock. Rising edge moves latch output to the output register.
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A→B path active; LOW = B→A path active.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Registered Bidirectional Transceiver
The 74x646 places two storage stages between the two bus ports: a latch (clock controlled by CLAB/CLBA) followed by a register (clocked by CLK). This gives a two stage pipeline for bus isolation useful in CPUs, DMA controllers, and memory interfaces where hold time or pipeline alignment is required.
Note: 74x646 uses clock controlled latches (CLAB/CLBA). 74x652 uses level controlled latches (LEAB/LEBA). For simpler applications without latching, the 74x245 or 74x645 is preferred.
74x647:
The 74x647 is the open collector version of the 74x646. Same registered bidirectional transceiver with CLAB/CLBA clock latches and CLK register, but with OC outputs. External pull ups required on all A/B port pins.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLAB: Clock Latch A-to B (rising edge).
  CLBA: Clock Latch B-to A (rising edge).
  A0: Port A bit 0 (OC).
  B0: Port B bit 0 (OC).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x646 vs 74x647
74x646 has tri state outputs; 74x647 has open collector. Choose 74x647 when the registered bus uses wire OR topology or drives optocouplers.
Note: External pull ups required on all A/B data pins.
74x648:
The 74x648 is a 24-pin registered bidirectional bus transceiver with inverting tri state outputs. Like the 74x646 but data is inverted through both pipeline stages. CLAB/CLBA are clock controlled latch enables; CLK clocks the output register.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLAB: Clock Latch A-to B (rising edge).
  CLBA: Clock Latch B-to A (rising edge).
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Inverting Registered Transceiver
Combining inversion with pipelining suits designs where one bus uses active LOW data convention and the other active HIGH, eliminating the need for separate inverter chips on the bus.
74x649:
The 74x649 is the open collector inverting version of the 74x648 registered transceiver. CLAB/CLBA clock controlled latches and CLK register with inverted data paths. External pull ups required on all A/B port pins.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLAB: Clock Latch A-to B (rising edge).
  CLBA: Clock Latch B-to A (rising edge).
  A0: Port A bit 0 (OC, inverted).
  B0: Port B bit 0 (OC, inverted).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Registered Transceiver Family (74x646 74x654)
  - 74x646/647: TRI/OC, non inv, CLAB/CLBA clock latch
  - 74x648/649: TRI/OC, inv, CLAB/CLBA clock latch
  - 74x651/652: TRI, inv/non inv, LEAB/LEBA level latch
  - 74x653/654: TRI+OC, inv/non inv, LEAB/LEBA level latch
74x651:
The 74x651 is a registered inverting bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. Unlike the 74x648, it uses level sensitive latch enables (LEAB/LEBA): when HIGH the latch is transparent; when LOW the data is held. CLK clocks the output register.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds data.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds data.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x648 vs 74x651
74x648 uses edge triggered clock controlled latches (CLAB/CLBA); 74x651 uses transparent latches (LEAB/LEBA). Both have CLK output register and inverting outputs. Use 74x651 when a level sensitive hold is preferred.
74x652:
The 74x652 is a registered non inverting bidirectional bus transceiver with tri state outputs. It uses level sensitive latches (LEAB/LEBA) and CLK output register. The non inverting complement of the 74x651; preferred when no data inversion is needed.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds data.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds data.
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x646 vs 74x652
74x646 uses clock controlled latches (CLAB/CLBA edge triggered); 74x652 uses level sensitive latches (LEAB/LEBA transparent). Both are non inverting tri state registered transceivers. Choose based on whether you need edge triggered or level triggered data capture.
74x653:
The 74x653 is a registered inverting bidirectional transceiver with mixed TRI/OC outputs: A-side tri state, B-side open collector. LEAB/LEBA level sensitive latches and CLK register. Bridges a tri state CPU bus to an OC backplane with data inversion.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds.
  A0: Port A bit 0 (tri state).
  B0: Port B bit 0 (open collector).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Mixed TRI/OC Registered Transceiver
A-side TRI outputs suit modern buses; B-side OC outputs suit legacy wire OR buses. The inverting path handles active LOW bus conventions.
Note: External pull ups required on B-side pins.
74x654:
The 74x654 is the non inverting version of the 74x653. Mixed TRI/OC outputs, LEAB/LEBA level latches, CLK register, bidirectional. Data passes without inversion through both pipeline stages.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  LEAB: Latch Enable A-to B. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds.
  LEBA: Latch Enable B-to A. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds.
  A0: Port A bit 0 (tri state).
  B0: Port B bit 0 (open collector).
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  CLK: Register clock (rising edge).
  DIR: Direction control.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x653 vs 74x654
74x653 inverts data; 74x654 is non inverting. Both use TRI A-side and OC B-side with LEAB/LEBA latches and CLK register.
74x655:
The 74x655 is an octal inverting line driver with integrated parity generation. It drives eight outputs (YA0-YA1 and YB0-YB7) with inverted copies of inputs A0-A7, and simultaneously computes a parity bit (PE). OEn controls the data outputs; PEOEn independently enables PE. PEIn contributes to the parity calculation.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable for data outputs (active LOW).
  A0: Data input bit 0.
  A1: Data input bit 1.
  A2: Data input bit 2.
  A3: Data input bit 3.
  A4: Data input bit 4.
  A5: Data input bit 5.
  A6: Data input bit 6.
  A7: Data input bit 7.
  PEIn: Parity input. Contributes to the parity calculation (allows cascading).
  PEOEn: Parity Output Enable (active LOW). Controls PE output.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  PE: Parity output. Odd/even parity over A0-A7 and PEIn.
  YB7: Inverted output of A7.
  YB6: Inverted output of A6.
  YB5: Inverted output of A5.
  YB4: Inverted output of A4.
  YB3: Inverted output of A3.
  YB2: Inverted output of A2.
  YB1: Inverted output of A1.
  YB0: Inverted output of A0.
  YA0: Inverted output of A0 (second copy).
  YA1: Inverted output of A1 (second copy).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Parity Generation
Parity generation XORs all data bits together. Even parity: PE is 1 when the number of 1s in the data is odd (making total count even). Odd parity: PE is 1 when the count is even. The PEIn pin allows cascading multiple 74x655 chips for wider data words.
Note: Parity tree is now evaluated: PE = XOR of A0-A7 ⊕ PEIn, gated by PEOEn for tri state. Cascading multiple chips via PEIn works as designed.
74x656:
The 74x656 is an octal non inverting line driver with integrated parity generation. Identical to the 74x655 except outputs are non inverted. OEn controls the data outputs; PEOEn controls the PE parity output. PEIn allows cascading for wider parity.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable for data outputs (active LOW).
  A0: Data input bit 0.
  A1: Data input bit 1.
  A2: Data input bit 2.
  A3: Data input bit 3.
  A4: Data input bit 4.
  A5: Data input bit 5.
  A6: Data input bit 6.
  A7: Data input bit 7.
  PEIn: Parity input for cascading.
  PEOEn: Parity Output Enable (active LOW).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  PE: Parity output.
  YB7: Non inverted output of A7.
  YB6: Non inverted output of A6.
  YB5: Non inverted output of A5.
  YB4: Non inverted output of A4.
  YB3: Non inverted output of A3.
  YB2: Non inverted output of A2.
  YB1: Non inverted output of A1.
  YB0: Non inverted output of A0.
  YA0: Non inverted output of A0 (second copy).
  YA1: Non inverted output of A1 (second copy).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x655 vs 74x656
74x655 inverts outputs; 74x656 is non inverting. Both generate a parity bit from the 8 data inputs and provide independent OEn for data and PEOEn for parity.
74x657:
The 74x657 combines an 8 bit bidirectional bus transceiver with an integrated parity checker/generator. In generate mode it computes parity of the data being driven; in check mode it verifies received data against an expected parity bit, asserting PE as an error flag. DIR and OEABn/OEBAn control direction.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  PEn: Parity Enable / mode select (active LOW).
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  PE: Parity Error output. HIGH indicates a parity mismatch.
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Transceiver with Parity
The 74x657 adds error detection to the standard bus transceiver. In memory bus applications, parity checking provides single bit error detection without the full cost of ECC.
Note: Parity output is now generated: PE = even parity of the active 8 bit bus (A side when DIR=HIGH, B side when DIR=LOW), gated by PEn.
74x658:
The 74x658 is an 8 bit inverting bus transceiver with integrated parity. It inverts data as it passes through and simultaneously generates/checks parity on the data word. OEABn/OEBAn control direction enables; DIR selects direction; PEn (active LOW) enables the parity function.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives inverted B; LOW = B drives inverted A.
  PEn: Parity Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 13).
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  PAR: Parity output. HIGH = parity error.
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x658/659/664/665 Parity Transceiver Family
  - 74x658: inverting, with parity
  - 74x659: non inverting, with parity
  - 74x664: inverting, with parity (alternate version)
  - 74x665: non inverting, with parity (alternate version)
Note: Parity is now generated: PAR = even parity of A0-A7 when PEn=LOW. Note that 74Sim only models the A→B path (drive B when OEABn=LOW and DIR=HIGH); the reverse B→A direction stays HiZ.
74x659:
The 74x659 is an 8 bit non inverting bus transceiver with integrated parity generation and checking. Data passes through without inversion; PAR reports a parity error. Complement of the 74x658.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).

  PEn: Parity Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Port A bit 0.

  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.

  B0: Port B bit 0.
  PAR: Parity output. HIGH = parity error.

Non Inverting Parity Transceiver
Adding parity to a standard transceiver lets the receiving end detect single bit transmission errors without extra logic. The PAR output can drive an interrupt line.
Note: Parity is now generated: PAR = even parity of A0-A7 when PEn=LOW. Note that 74Sim only models the A→B path (drive B when OEABn=LOW and DIR=HIGH); the reverse B→A direction stays HiZ.
74x664:
The 74x664 is an 8 bit inverting parity transceiver, an alternate variant of the 74x658. Both are ALS family inverting transceivers with parity generation/checking, differing in minor interface details or speed grades.

  B0: Port B bit 0.
  PAR: Parity output. HIGH = parity error.
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x658 vs 74x664
Both 74x658 and 74x664 are ALS family inverting parity transceivers. Verify the datasheet for the exact speed grade and family variant needed for your design.
Note: Parity is now generated: PAR = even parity of A0-A7 when PEn=LOW. Note that 74Sim only models the A→B path (drive B when OEABn=LOW and DIR=HIGH); the reverse B→A direction stays HiZ.
74x665:
The 74x665 is an 8 bit non inverting parity transceiver, an alternate variant of the 74x659. Both are ALS family non inverting transceivers with parity generation/checking.

Pins:
  OEABn: A-to B Output Enable (active LOW).
  OEBAn: B-to A Output Enable (active LOW).
  DIR: Direction. HIGH = A drives B; LOW = B drives A.
  PEn: Parity Enable (active LOW).
  A0: Port A bit 0.
  A1: Port A bit 1.
  A2: Port A bit 2.
  A3: Port A bit 3.
  A4: Port A bit 4.
  A5: Port A bit 5.
  A6: Port A bit 6.
  A7: Port A bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 13).
  B7: Port B bit 7.
  B6: Port B bit 6.
  B5: Port B bit 5.
  B4: Port B bit 4.
  B3: Port B bit 3.
  B2: Port B bit 2.
  B1: Port B bit 1.
  B0: Port B bit 0.
  PAR: Parity output.
  NC: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

74x659 vs 74x665
Both 74x659 and 74x665 are non inverting parity transceivers in the ALS family. Check the specific datasheet for propagation delay and power differences.
Note: Parity is now generated: PAR = even parity of A0-A7 when PEn=LOW. Note that 74Sim only models the A→B path (drive B when OEABn=LOW and DIR=HIGH); the reverse B→A direction stays HiZ.
74x666:
The 74x666 is an 8 bit transparent D type latch with tri state outputs, a CLR (active LOW) input, and a read-back capability. When LE is HIGH the outputs follow the data inputs; when LE goes LOW the data is held. OE (active LOW) enables the tri state outputs. CLR asynchronously resets all bits to 0.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). Pull HIGH to tri state all outputs.
  CLR: Asynchronous clear (active LOW). Resets all latched data to 0.
  LE: Latch Enable. HIGH = transparent (Q follows D); LOW = holds data.
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q7: Latched output bit 7.
  Q6: Latched output bit 6.
  Q5: Latched output bit 5.
  Q4: Latched output bit 4.
  Q3: Latched output bit 3.
  Q2: Latched output bit 2.
  Q1: Latched output bit 1.
  Q0: Latched output bit 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

8 bit Transparent Latch with Clear
The 74x666 is similar to the 74x573 but adds a CLR input. On a microprocessor bus, CLR can power on reset the latch to a known state without requiring a separate write cycle.
74x667:
The 74x667 is the inverting version of the 74x666. Same 8 bit transparent latch with LE, OE (active LOW), and CLR (active LOW), but outputs are inverted copies of the stored data. Useful when driving active LOW devices from an active HIGH data bus.

Pins:
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  CLR: Asynchronous clear (active LOW).
  LE: Latch Enable. HIGH = transparent; LOW = holds data.
  D0: Data input bit 0.
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  D5: Data input bit 5.
  D6: Data input bit 6.
  D7: Data input bit 7.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 12).
  Q7: Inverted output bit 7.
  Q6: Inverted output bit 6.
  Q5: Inverted output bit 5.
  Q4: Inverted output bit 4.
  Q3: Inverted output bit 3.
  Q2: Inverted output bit 2.
  Q1: Inverted output bit 1.
  Q0: Inverted output bit 0.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 24).

Inverting 8 bit Latch
The Q output is the logical complement of the stored D value. Storing HIGH on D produces LOW on Q, inverting the polarity of the entire 8 bit word at the output.
74x668:
The 74x668 is a synchronous presettable 4 bit BCD (decade) up/down counter. It counts 0-9 in BCD, rolling over at 9 (up) or 0 (down). UD selects direction; LOAD presets the count from A-D inputs on the next clock edge; ENP and ENT enable counting and drive the ripple carry output RCO.

Pins:
  CLK: Clock input. Counter advances on the rising edge.
  UD: Up/Down select. HIGH = count up; LOW = count down.
  A: Preset data input bit A (LSB).
  B: Preset data input bit B.
  C: Preset data input bit C.
  D: Preset data input bit D (MSB).
  ENP: Enable P (active HIGH). Must be HIGH with ENT to count.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output (active LOW). Asserts at terminal count to enable cascading.
  QD: Counter output bit D (MSB).
  QC: Counter output bit C.
  QB: Counter output bit B.
  QA: Counter output bit A (LSB).
  ENT: Enable T (active HIGH). Must be HIGH with ENP to count. Also drives RCO.
  LOAD: Synchronous Parallel Load (active LOW). Loads A-D on the next rising clock edge.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

BCD Counting
BCD counting uses 4 bits but only counts 0 through 9. This is ideal for driving 7 segment displays or decimal arithmetic without a binary to decimal conversion step.

Cascading
Connect RCO of a lower stage to ENT of the next stage to cascade decade counters into a multi digit counter. ENP must be HIGH on all stages.
74x669:
The 74x669 is a synchronous presettable 4 bit binary up/down counter. Counts 0 15 in binary. Identical control interface to the 74x668 (UD, LOAD, ENP, ENT, RCO) but counts in binary rather than BCD.

Pins:
  CLK: Clock input. Counter advances on the rising edge.
  UD: Up/Down select. HIGH = count up; LOW = count down.
  A: Preset data input bit A (LSB).
  B: Preset data input bit B.
  C: Preset data input bit C.
  D: Preset data input bit D (MSB).
  ENP: Enable P (active HIGH). Must be HIGH with ENT to count.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RCO: Ripple Carry Output (active LOW). Asserts at terminal count (0 or 15).
  QD: Counter output bit D (MSB).
  QC: Counter output bit C.
  QB: Counter output bit B.
  QA: Counter output bit A (LSB).
  ENT: Enable T (active HIGH). Must be HIGH with ENP to count.
  LOAD: Synchronous Parallel Load (active LOW).
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

74x668 (BCD) vs 74x669 (Binary)
74x668 counts 0-9; 74x669 counts 0-15. Use 74x668 for decimal display drivers; use 74x669 for binary address generation, timers, or general purpose counting.
74x670:
The 74x670 is a 4 word × 4 bit register file with independent read and write ports and tri state outputs. Write address (WA1/WA2) selects which of four registers to write; WE clocks the data in. Read address (RA1/RA2) and RE select and enable the output. Both ports are independent, allowing simultaneous read and write of different registers.

Pins:
  D1: Data input bit 1.
  D2: Data input bit 2.
  D3: Data input bit 3.
  D4: Data input bit 4.
  WA1: Write address bit 1 (LSB).
  WA2: Write address bit 2 (MSB).
  WE: Write Enable (active LOW). Stores D1-D4 into the selected register.
  GND: Ground reference (pin 8).
  RA2: Read address bit 2 (MSB).
  RA1: Read address bit 1 (LSB).
  RE: Read Enable / Output Enable (active LOW). Enables Q1-Q4.
  Q4: Register file output bit 4.
  Q3: Register file output bit 3.
  Q2: Register file output bit 2.
  Q1: Register file output bit 1.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 16).

Register File Concept
A register file is a small multi port memory: you can write to one address while reading from a different address simultaneously. The 74x670 stores 4 words × 4 bits = 16 bits total.
Cascade two 74x670s with shared address lines and separate data/output bits for 4 × 8 bit registers.
74x671:
The 74x671 is a 4 bit bidirectional shift register with parallel load, mode select (S0/S1), tri state outputs, and asynchronous (direct) CLR. S0/S1 select among shift left, shift right, parallel load, and hold modes. SI is the serial input; SO is the serial output end. CLR asynchronously resets all bits.

Pins:
  S0: Mode select bit 0. With S1 selects shift/load/hold mode.
  S1: Mode select bit 1.
  SI: Serial Input for shifting.
  A: Parallel data input bit A.
  B: Parallel data input bit B.
  C: Parallel data input bit C.
  D: Parallel data input bit D.
  CLR: Asynchronous Clear (active LOW). Immediately resets QA-QD to 0.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock input. Shift/load/hold occurs on rising edge.
  QD: Registered output bit D.
  QC: Registered output bit C.
  QB: Registered output bit B.
  QA: Registered output bit A.
  SO: Serial Output (last stage output).
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Shift Register Modes
  S1=0, S0=0 → Hold (no change)
  S1=0, S0=1 → Shift right (SI→QA→QB...)
  S1=1, S0=0 → Shift left (...QC→QD→SO)
  S1=1, S0=1 → Parallel load (A-D → QA-QD)

74x671 vs 74x672
74x671 has an asynchronous (direct) CLR; 74x672 has a synchronous CLR that only clears on the next clock edge.
74x672:
The 74x672 is identical to the 74x671 except CLR is synchronous: it clears on the next rising clock edge rather than immediately. Synchronous clear improves noise immunity and ensures glitch free reset in timing critical circuits.

Pins:
  S0: Mode select bit 0.
  S1: Mode select bit 1.
  SI: Serial Input.
  A: Parallel data input A.
  B: Parallel data input B.
  C: Parallel data input C.
  D: Parallel data input D.
  CLR: Synchronous Clear (active LOW). Clears on the next rising clock edge.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW).
  GND: Ground reference (pin 10).
  CLK: Clock input (rising edge).
  QD: Output bit D.
  QC: Output bit C.
  QB: Output bit B.
  QA: Output bit A.
  SO: Serial Output.
  NC1: No connect.
  NC2: No connect.
  NC3: No connect.
  VCC: Positive supply (+5 V, pin 20).

Synchronous vs Asynchronous Clear
Synchronous clear waits for the clock edge to take effect, preventing unwanted glitches when CLR and CLK transitions overlap. Use 74x672 in high speed or noise sensitive systems.
74x673:
The 74x673 is a 16 bit serial in, parallel out (SIPO) shift register with a separate output storage register. SER clocks data in on CLK; RCK transfers the shift register contents to the output latch, which drives Q0-Q15. OEn enables the tri state parallel outputs. SOUT provides daisy chain serial output.

Pins:
  SER: Serial data input. Shifted in on each CLK rising edge.
  CLK: Shift clock. Rising edge shifts SER into the register.
  RCK: Register clock. Rising edge transfers shift register to output latch.
  OEn: Output Enable (active LOW). Enables Q0-Q15 parallel outputs.
  Q15: Parallel output bit 15 (most recently shifted bit).
  Q14: Parallel output bit 14.
  Q13: Parallel output bit 13.
  Q12: Parallel output bit 12.
  Q11: Parallel output bit 11.
  guideOverview: The 74x675 is a 16 bit SIPO shift register that directly drives Q0-Q15 outputs from the shift stages (no separate output latch). SER enters data on CLK; OEn enables all outputs. Unlike the 74x673, there is no RCK so outputs update live with each clock cycle.
